title: Pipeline screen (UI screen 6) entirely missing — no agent tree, per-agent model/temp/prompt config, or traces
evidence: Spec §17 screen 6: 'Pipeline — view the agent tree; per-agent config (model, temperature, prompt version); run-now; recent traces.' Sidebar nav (sidebar.tsx:36-46) lists only Dashboard/Digests/News/Sources/Watchlist/Settings — no Pipeline route exists under frontend/app. API has no /pipeline/config route (grep of app/api/app.py decorators returns dashboard/runs/news/sources/watchlist/settings only; spec §17 promised GET/PUT /api/pipeline/config). Per-agent config is impossible: every stage agent is built from a SINGLE global setting — critic.py:69 build_critic_agent(settings.llm_model, settings.llm_temperature), processing.py / digest_editor.py:42 / judge.py:68 all pass settings.llm_model + settings.llm_temperature; there is no per-agent override path anywhere. The pipeline also computes audit data (run.critic_verdicts / run.flagged, agents.py:359-360) that is persisted but has no API serializer or screen to surface it.
impact: The product gives the user no way to see or tune the agents — exactly the 'the agents feel disconnected from the webapp' sensation. The guardrail/critic audit trail the backend works hard to produce is invisible. The single most spec-divergent screen.
recommendation: Either build a minimal Pipeline screen (read-only agent tree + the existing per-run critic verdicts/flagged, plus a global model/temperature editor wired to Settings) and add GET /api/pipeline/config, OR formally DROP screen 6 from the v1 promise in spec/ARCHITECTURE. Do not leave it as an unstated gap. If keeping per-agent config in spec, it requires a real config model (the code currently has only one global model/temp).
verifier_notes:
CONFIRMED. The Pipeline screen (spec screen 6) is entirely missing and the finding's core is accurate.

EVIDENCE VERIFIED:
- Spec basis is real. docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-23-adk-catchup-agent-design.md:238 states verbatim: "6. Pipeline — view the agent tree; per-agent config (model, temperature, prompt version); run-now; recent traces." Line 248 promises "GET/PUT /api/pipeline/config". FR7 (line 46) lists "pipeline view" as a console requirement.
- No Pipeline route: frontend/app/ contains only digests, news, settings, sources, watchlist + root dashboard page.tsx. No pipeline dir.
- Sidebar (frontend/components/layout/sidebar.tsx:32-49) lists exactly 6 nav items: Dashboard, Digests, News, Sources, Watchlist, Settings. No Pipeline link. (Finding cited lines 36-46; actual NAV_GROUPS spans 32-49 — minor line-number drift, content correct.)
- No /pipeline/config route. Full @api decorator list in app/api/app.py: GET /health; GET/PUT /settings; GET /dashboard; GET /runs, GET /runs/{id}, POST /runs; GET /news; GET/PUT /sources, POST /sources/resolve; GET/PUT /watchlist. (Note: POST /runs at app.py:273 DOES provide the spec's "run-now" capability at the API level — but no screen exposes it as a pipeline action.)
- Per-agent config absent for runtime stages: critic.py:69, processing.py:64 & 112, digest_editor.py:42 all build agents from the single global settings.llm_model + settings.llm_temperature. No per-agent model/temp/prompt-version override path for these stages, and no UI/API to set them.
- Audit data persisted but unsurfaced: app/core/domain.py:186-187 DigestRun has flagged:int and critic_verdicts:list[dict]; agents.py:359-360 populates them. Frontend grep for critic/verdict/flagged returns zero real usage. (Caveat: RunDetail at schemas.py:17-19 embeds the full DigestRun, so the API technically serializes critic_verdicts/flagged via GET /runs/{id} — but no screen renders them, so they are effectively invisible to the user, matching the finding's impact.)

ONE OVERSTATEMENT (does not change verdict): The finding asserts "there is no per-agent override path anywhere." This is factually wrong — config.py:92 defines judge_model: str | None and judge.py:67 uses `settings.judge_model or settings.llm_model`, a genuine per-agent model override. However, the judge is an OFFLINE eval-time component (ADK-GUIDE.md:63), not one of the runtime pipeline stages, so the spirit of the claim (runtime stages have no per-agent config) still holds.

Severity high is justified: an entire spec-promised console screen plus its backing API contract are absent, and the guardrail/critic audit trail the backend produces has no UI surface.

title: Product API is open-by-default and that default ships into the deployed Cloud Run container
evidence: config.py:138 api_key defaults None (auth off). app.py:104-105 returns no-op when unset. fast_api_app.py:50,77 registers product routes with no api_key enforcement, so the Cloud Run container (Dockerfile uvicorn app.fast_api_app:app on 0.0.0.0) serves dashboard, runs GET+POST trigger, news, sources GET+PUT, watchlist GET+PUT with zero auth unless API_KEY is set. Warning log app.py:151-155, nothing fails closed.
impact: Deployed without API_KEY (default; nothing sets it), the API is world read+write: overwrite the source list via PUT sources (injecting arbitrary URLs the server then fetches as a proxy/amplifier even within the SSRF allowlist), overwrite watchlist, trigger runs, read all news.
recommendation: Fail closed for any non-loopback bind: refuse to start (or auto-enforce) when app_host is non-loopback and api_key is unset. For fast_api_app.py require API_KEY (raise at import). Document API_KEY as a required deploy env var.
verifier_notes:
Confirmed against source. config.py:138 `api_key: str | None = None` with inline comment "leaves the API open (local/dev default)". app.py:93-111 `_require_api_key` returns no-op when `settings.api_key` is falsy (line 104-105 `if not settings.api_key: return`). app.py:151-155 only emits a logger.warning when unset — nothing fails closed. fast_api_app.py:77 calls `register_product_routes(app, _settings)`, attaching all /api/* product routes (GET /dashboard, /runs, /runs/{id}, /news, /sources, /watchlist; PUT /sources, /watchlist; POST /runs, /sources/resolve) onto the deployed ADK container. Dockerfile CMD runs `uvicorn app.fast_api_app:app --host 0.0.0.0` (port 8080, NOT 8000 as the finding stated — 8000 is only the __main__ fallback at fast_api_app.py:98; immaterial to the vuln). No in-repo gate fails this closed: there are no Terraform files (find -name '*.tf' empty), no deployment/infra dir, manifest deployment_target: "none", and nothing sets API_KEY (only blank placeholders in app/.env.example:13 and frontend/.env.local.example). So the open default genuinely reaches the container unless an operator manually sets API_KEY.

Corrections / overstatements in the finding: (1) port is 8080 not 8000. (2) The "proxy/amplifier" SSRF angle is bounded: SourceConfig._reject_dangerous_scheme (config.py:64-74) restricts source URLs to http/https, and POST /runs + POST /sources/resolve are rate-limited via TokenBucket (app.py:273,283-284) — the finding acknowledges the allowlist but should note the rate cap. (3) The Settings surface (GET/PUT /settings) is NOT part of the unauthenticated exposure: it is separately gated by _require_local_write (app.py:63-78: loopback IP + loopback Host + loopback Origin/Referer checks), so secret-writing endpoints are protected even with api_key unset. The data routes (sources/watchlist/runs/news/dashboard) are the actual open surface, which matches the impact claim. Core finding holds: open-by-default API ships into the deployed Cloud Run container with no fail-closed mechanism; world read + write to config the server later fetches, plus trigger runs. Severity high is appropriate.

title: No CI anywhere: 415 backend tests + 82 frontend tests + the eval regression gate are never run automatically
evidence: `ls .github .gitlab .circleci` -> all absent ('NO .github DIR'); no workflow yml/yaml found anywhere outside frontend/node_modules. The manifest's cicd_runner is 'skip'. So the otherwise-excellent suite (verified: 414 passed/1 skipped in 3.2s backend; 82 passed frontend) only ever runs when a human types the command. The eval regression gate (scripts/eval_enrichment.py check_regression, tested in test_eval_harness.py) and the committed baseline.json are pure documentation with no enforcement.
impact: A green test suite is only ever a local, manual assertion. Any push/merge can silently ship a regression — a broken Zod schema, a drifted prompt-vs-schema contract, a storage contract break — because nothing gates it. For a public repo this also means external contributors' PRs are untested at the door. The investment in 497 real tests is under-leveraged: the safety net exists but is never deployed.
recommendation: Add a single .github/workflows/ci.yml that runs `uv run pytest tests/unit tests/integration` and `cd frontend && npm ci && npm run test` and `npm run build` on push + PR. Optionally add the offline eval gate (run_eval with the fake judge is deterministic) so the regression baseline is actually enforced. This is the highest-leverage test-quality fix in the repo.
verifier_notes:
Every cited fact holds up under direct inspection.

NO CI: .github, .gitlab, .circleci all absent (confirmed via both `ls` and `git ls-files`). The only YAML files tracked are agents-cli-manifest.yaml, config/sources.yaml, config/watchlist.yaml — none are CI workflows. No .husky, no .pre-commit-config.yaml, no lefthook — i.e. zero automation layer of any kind, not just no GitHub Actions.

MANIFEST: /agents-cli-manifest.yaml line 11 `cicd_runner: "skip"` (and line 9 `deployment_target: "none"`) confirmed verbatim.

TEST COUNTS: backend = exactly 415 tests collected via `uv run pytest tests/unit tests/integration --collect-only` (matches claim). frontend = exactly 82 test cases counted via `it(`/`test(` across frontend/app|components|lib (matches claim exactly). frontend/package.json has `"test": "vitest run"` but it is manual-only.

EVAL GATE: scripts/eval_enrichment.py defines check_regression (line 145), load_baseline/save_baseline, and a --check-regression CLI flag documented to "EXIT NON-ZERO if any" regression vs the committed tests/eval/baseline.json (file exists). The gate logic is real and tested (tests/unit/test_eval_harness.py exists) — but nothing invokes it on push/merge, so it is, as claimed, unenforced documentation.

IMPACT is fairly stated: the 497-test suite plus the regression gate only ever run when a human types the command; any push/merge can ship a regression silently and external contributors' PRs are untested at the door.

Severity kept at high (not downgraded). This is a category=deployment process gap, not a runtime exploit, but for a public repo with a large existing suite and an unenforced regression gate, "high" is defensible — the safety net exists yet gates nothing. The only nuance worth noting is that the underlying tests genuinely pass; the defect is purely the absence of automated enforcement, which is exactly what the finding asserts.

title: Layering inversion: low-level pipeline imports orchestration from high-level runner, forming a module cycle
evidence: app/pipeline/agents.py:47-54 does `from app.runner import (_collect, _default_critic, _default_narrator, _default_processor, _default_reprocessor, select_rendered)`. Meanwhile app/runner.py:26-27 imports `from app.pipeline import critic, digest_editor, processing` AND app/runner.py:158 imports `from app.pipeline.agents import build_pipeline`. runner.py is the high-level use-case (it owns run_digest, build_storage, make_session_service, the DI default factories) yet the pipeline node module reaches UP into it for collector dispatch (_collect) and stage default factories. The cycle is real: runner.py:155-158 has a comment admitting it — 'app.pipeline.agents imports this module at top level, so importing it here at module scope would reintroduce a load-time cycle' — and resolves it with a deferred in-function import. Smoke test confirms both modules only co-import because of that deferral.
impact: This is the single clearest piece of evidence for the 'scattered' feeling at the boundary level. Dependencies flow both ways between the orchestration layer and the agent-tree layer, so neither can be reasoned about or reused in isolation, and the cycle is a latent import-order trap (any new top-level import of runner from pipeline, or vice-versa, will break startup). It directly contradicts the spec's claim that 'each layer talks to the next through a narrow interface'.
recommendation: Break the cycle by moving the collector dispatch (_collect) and the stage default factories (_default_processor/_narrator/_critic/_reprocessor) OUT of runner.py into the layer the pipeline already depends on — e.g. a new app/pipeline/collectors.py (dispatch) and app/pipeline/defaults.py (factories), or the spec's core/usecases/. Then pipeline imports downward only, and runner.py composes pipeline + storage + session at the top. This removes the deferred-import workaround entirely.
verifier_notes:
All factual claims verified by direct file reads. app/pipeline/agents.py:47-54 has a TOP-LEVEL `from app.runner import (_collect, _default_critic, _default_narrator, _default_processor, _default_reprocessor, select_rendered)`. app/runner.py imports the pipeline back: line 26 `from app.pipeline import critic as critic_module`, line 27 `from app.pipeline import digest_editor, processing`, and line 158 `from app.pipeline.agents import build_pipeline` (deferred, inside run_digest). The cycle is real and explicitly acknowledged by the comment at runner.py:155-158 ("app.pipeline.agents imports this module at top level, so importing it here at module scope would reintroduce a load-time cycle"), which is resolved via the in-function deferred import. runner.py does own the high-level use-case surface (run_digest, build_storage, make_session_service) plus the DI default factories and _collect/select_rendered, and the low-level node module reaches UP into it — so the layering inversion is genuine.

Severity corrected from high to medium for two reasons: (1) The coupled surface from pipeline->runner is narrow and consists of misplaced leaf utilities (_collect, select_rendered) and DI default-factory closures (_default_processor etc.) — these are stateless helpers that arguably belong in a shared/lower layer, NOT deep orchestration logic; the only runner->pipeline edge is the build_pipeline factory used in-function. It is a latent import-order maintainability trap, not a live defect (it works correctly today via the deferral). (2) The impact statement quotes the spec as saying "each layer talks to the next through a narrow interface" — that literal phrase does NOT exist in the repo docs (grep of docs/ADK-GUIDE.md and BUILD-LOG.md found no such wording; closest is ADK-GUIDE.md:148-149 "injectable-boundary pattern"), so the "directly contradicts the spec" framing rests on a paraphrase rather than a literal spec claim. Real architecture-drift finding, but overstated as high.

title: "Swap APScheduler -> Cloud Scheduler by config alone" is false — no port, no adapter, no config knob
evidence: ARCHITECTURE.md:13-14 states the system can 'swap SQLite -> Firestore, AI Studio -> Vertex AI, or APScheduler -> Cloud Scheduler by config alone.' For the scheduler this is unbackable: app/services/scheduler.py:18-36 hardcodes `from apscheduler.schedulers.background import BackgroundScheduler` with no interface to swap against; app/core/config.py:120-122 exposes only schedule_enabled / schedule_cron / schedule_timezone — there is NO scheduler_backend knob (grep confirms zero matches for cloud_scheduler/scheduler_backend). Switching to Cloud Scheduler would require rewriting scheduler.py and the lifespan wiring, not flipping config. (By contrast storage_backend in config.py:whole-file and use_vertexai at config.py:114 + configure_genai at llm/runtime.py:36-54 ARE real config-only swaps — so the doc over-promises only on the scheduler.)
impact: A reader trusting ARCHITECTURE.md will believe production scheduling is a config change away; it is a code-rewrite. This is precisely the kind of doc-vs-reality gap that makes the project feel like it's 'not behaving the way it's supposed to.'
recommendation: Either introduce a Scheduler port + LocalScheduler/CloudScheduler adapters + a scheduler_backend config (to make the claim true), or amend ARCHITECTURE.md:13-14 to say only SQLite->Firestore and AIStudio->Vertex are config-swappable and that Cloud Scheduler is a future/manual migration.
verifier_notes: Finding holds on every checkable claim. ARCHITECTURE.md:13-15 literally groups three swaps as "by config alone": SQLite->Firestore, AI Studio->Vertex AI, APScheduler->Cloud Scheduler. The first two are genuinely config-only port/adapter swaps: app/core/ports/storage.py defines an abstract StorageBackend ABC with SqliteBackend/FirestoreBackend adapters and build_storage() (app/runner.py:52-62) dispatches on settings.storage_backend; configure_genai() (app/llm/runtime.py:36-48) reads use_vertexai (config.py:114) to set GOOGLE_GENAI_USE_VERTEXAI. The scheduler swap, by contrast, is NOT config-only: app/services/scheduler.py hardcodes `from apscheduler.schedulers.background import BackgroundScheduler` (line 26) + CronTrigger (line 27) with no scheduler port/ABC/adapter; config.py:120-122 exposes only schedule_enabled/schedule_cron/schedule_timezone (none selects a backend); grep for scheduler_backend/cloud_scheduler/scheduler_provider returns ZERO matches in app/. The lifespan in app/api/app.py:337-349 wires APScheduler's .start()/.shutdown() directly. Switching to Cloud Scheduler requires code, not config. Severity corrected high->medium because this is doc-drift with a real, documented production path: config.py:119 comment says "Cloud deploys instead point Cloud Scheduler at POST /api/runs (see docs)" — so Cloud Scheduler IS supported in prod, just by an external scheduler hitting the existing /api/runs endpoint rather than flipping an app knob. Only the "by config alone" phrasing (which implies a symmetric adapter swap) is inaccurate; the reader is not left without a migration path.

title: No single Settings entry point: Settings() constructed 4x, two at import time — the mechanical source of the 'config is everywhere' feeling
evidence: Settings() is instantiated independently at app/agent.py:24 (import time), app/fast_api_app.py:50 (import time), app/runner.py:160 (per run_digest call), and app/api/app.py:324. There is no cached/singleton get_settings() factory in app/core/config.py. Worse, app/agent.py:24-25 does Settings() AND build_storage() AND build_pipeline() at MODULE IMPORT TIME, so merely importing app.agent triggers DB schema init + watchlist/source loading as a side effect.
impact: Four independent config reads (two at import) mean there is no one place that owns configuration, and the import-time side effects make the dependency graph fragile (importing the agent module mutates the filesystem). Together these create the diffuse 'config and setup are scattered across the codebase' sensation even though config.py itself is well-written.
recommendation: Add a cached `get_settings()` (functools.lru_cache) in core/config.py and route all four call sites through it. Move the import-time work in agent.py:24-25 into a build_root_agent() function called lazily by the entrypoints, eliminating the import-time DB/IO side effect.
verifier_notes:
Core claims verified against source. grep confirms exactly four Settings() instantiations and no others (excluding tests): app/agent.py:24, app/fast_api_app.py:50, app/runner.py:160, app/api/app.py:324. Two are pure module-level/import-time (agent.py:24 and fast_api_app.py:50 both `_settings = Settings()` at module scope). The other two are inside functions using the injectable `settings or Settings()` fallback (runner.py:160 in run_digest, api/app.py:324 in create_app) — so they are NOT equivalent to the import-time ones; they only construct a default when no settings is passed. No cached/singleton get_settings() factory exists: no lru_cache/@cache anywhere, and the only `get_settings` (app/api/app.py:171) is a FastAPI route handler returning a dict, not a Settings factory.

Import-time side effects of app/agent.py are real and the strongest part of the finding: line 25 runs build_storage(_settings) -> store.init_schema() which does sqlite3.connect(self.path) + CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS (app/adapters/storage/sqlite_backend.py:42-61), creating the DB file on disk merely by importing app.agent. It also runs build_pipeline(...) -> load_watchlist(settings.config_dir) (app/pipeline/agents.py:482) at build time, reading watchlist config at import.

One overstatement to correct: the evidence says import triggers "watchlist/source loading." Watchlist loading at import is confirmed, but SOURCE loading is NOT an import-time side effect — load_sources (app/pipeline/agents.py:201) lives inside SourceCollectorAgent._run_async_impl and only fires during pipeline execution, not at build_pipeline time. So the import-time side effects are: SQLite schema init (filesystem write) + watchlist read, not source loading.

Severity medium is correct: genuine architecture drift (no single config owner, import-time filesystem touch makes the dependency graph fragile and import-order-sensitive, harms testability of app.agent), but not a correctness/security defect — init_schema is idempotent (CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS) and config injection is supported at every call site that runs in production paths (runner/api accept settings). Verdict confirmed because the four counts, the two-at-import claim, the no-singleton claim, and the import-time DB+watchlist side effects all hold; only the "source loading" sub-claim is inaccurate, which is a minor refinement, not a refutation.

title: Importing any app submodule builds a pipeline and a SQLite DB at import time
evidence: app/__init__.py:15 imports .agent; app/agent.py:24-26 runs build_pipeline+build_storage at import; build_storage runner.py:61 calls init_schema. Verified empirically: importing app.core.config loads app.agent/runner/pipeline and creates the SQLite file on disk.
impact: DB file+migration on any import; stale 2nd root_agent; import failures crash imports; test collection pollutes data/catchup.db.
recommendation: Make root_agent lazy; drop the agent import from app/__init__.py.
verifier_notes:
Core mechanism is REAL and empirically reproduced. app/__init__.py:15 does `from .agent import app`, so importing ANY app submodule first runs the package __init__ → app/agent.py:24-26 instantiates Settings() and runs `build_pipeline(_settings, build_storage(_settings), run_id=...)` at module scope → runner.py:52-62 build_storage() calls store.init_schema(); SqliteBackend.__init__ (sqlite_backend.py:19-21) mkdir's the parent and init_schema (42-63) runs CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS + _ensure_columns ALTERs + CREATE INDEX. 

Verified empirically in clean temp dirs: (a) `import app.core.config` creates the SQLite file and loads app.agent/app.runner/app.pipeline.agents; (b) even a pure leaf `import app.core.domain` creates data/catchup.db and loads app.agent; (c) with NO env override, `from app import runner` creates the REAL repo-default data/catchup.db plus -shm/-wal WAL files. sessions.db is NOT created at import time (deferred to run_digest/make_session_service), so the side effect is the app DB only.

Test-pollution sub-claim CONFIRMED: there is no conftest.py anywhere and neither .env nor app/.env sets SQLITE_PATH, while 5 test files do `from app import runner` (tests/integration/test_run_digest*.py, tests/unit/test_session_service.py). Collection thus writes data/catchup.db (default REPO_ROOT/data path, config.py:82) plus WAL files. data/ is gitignored (.gitignore:41), so it isn't committed, but it is still mutated on disk during test runs.

"import failures crash imports": TRUE in principle — any error in Settings()/build_storage/build_pipeline at import time fails every app submodule import (classic import-time-side-effect fragility).

The ONE overstated sub-claim is "stale 2nd root_agent". The module-level root_agent/app in app/agent.py is NOT stale/dead leftover scaffold — it is the intentional ADK entrypoint for `adk run`/`adk web`, resolved via agents-cli-manifest.yaml (agent_directory: "app") and documented in docs/ADK-GUIDE.md. The runtime path (run_digest in runner.py:146-198) deliberately builds a fresh per-run tree with its own run_id; the import-time tree is simply unused by that path, by design — not a wasteful duplicate bug. The design doc (docs/superpowers/plans/2026-05-24-plan8-orchestration.md:79) explicitly acknowledged "Import-time build_storage opens sqlite — acceptable for adk web; note lazy-init as a follow-up", so the import-time DB open was a KNOWN, accepted tradeoff, not an undiscovered defect.

Severity corrected high→medium: the import-time side effects (DB file/migration on any import, test-collection disk pollution, import fragility) are genuine code-hygiene/correctness smells worth fixing (move build to lazy/factory), but there is no data loss, the gitignored DB is not committed, normal CLI/API/adk-web flows work, and the "stale second root_agent" framing is inaccurate. Real but partly overstated → medium.

title: Firestore backend is unproven against any real store — config swap would fail on first query (no composite indexes, deprecated where())
evidence: firestore_backend.py:65,67,90-95 build queries with positional where(field, op, value) and order_by('collected_at', 'DESCENDING'); list_news combines an equality filter (category/importance/is_flagged) WITH order_by on a different field — real Firestore requires a composite index for this and returns FAILED_PRECONDITION (HTTP 400) without one. The adapter's own docstring (lines 5-11) admits positional where() is deprecated and must be swapped to FieldFilter before deploy. fake_firestore.py:1-4 states explicitly it has 'no real query semantics (no composite-index or inequality-filter constraints), so it validates the adapter's logic, not the backing store itself.' The only real-store gate, test_firestore_emulator.py:23-29, is pytestmark.skip(...) and its single test body raises AssertionError('implement against the emulator before deploy'). pyproject.toml:42-43 gates google-cloud-firestore behind an optional [firestore] extra that is not installed in the test env (test_build_storage.py:30-37 skips when it IS installed).
impact: The headline 'swap SQLite -> Firestore by config only, no rewrite' promise (spec swap-points, ARCHITECTURE.md, README prod path) is undelivered. Setting STORAGE_BACKEND=firestore in production would construct a client, pass all offline tests, then throw a missing-index error the first time the dashboard/news/digests pages issue a filtered+ordered query — i.e. the entire product UI would be broken in prod. This is a core contributor to the user's 'agents and gcp webapp and sqlite all scattered' feeling: the prose advertises a Firestore prod path that does not actually work.
recommendation: Pick ONE: (a) Fund Firestore for real before claiming it works — un-skip and implement test_firestore_emulator.py against gcloud beta emulators firestore, swap positional where() to where(filter=FieldFilter(...)), declare the composite indexes (category+collected_at, importance+collected_at, is_flagged+collected_at) in firestore.indexes.json, and run the StorageContract against the emulator in CI; OR (b) explicitly label Firestore experimental/unsupported in README + ARCHITECTURE and drop it from the 'config-only swap' marketing until (a) is done. Do not ship it as a silent config option that 404s on first use.
verifier_notes:
The core technical claims are accurate, but the framing ("undelivered", "entire product UI broken in prod", "would fail on first query") is overstated because the limitation is explicitly documented and gated.

CONFIRMED facts:
- firestore_backend.py:88-95 list_news combines an equality where() (category/importance/is_flagged) with order_by("collected_at","DESCENDING") on a DIFFERENT field. Real Firestore requires a manually created composite index for this and returns FAILED_PRECONDITION without one. This is genuine and would fail in prod on filtered news queries.
- firestore_backend.py:65-67 uses positional where(field, op, value); docstring lines 5-8 admit positional where is deprecated and must become FieldFilter before deploy.
- fake_firestore.py:1-4 + FakeQuery.stream (51-83) confirm zero index/inequality enforcement — validates adapter logic only, not the store.
- The only live-store gate, test_firestore_emulator.py:23-29, is pytest.mark.skip and the test body raises AssertionError("implement against the emulator before deploy").
- pyproject.toml:42-43 gates google-cloud-firestore behind optional [firestore] extra not installed in tests (test_build_storage.py:30-37 confirms).

WHERE THE FINDING IS OVERSTATED (why partial, severity down to medium):
1. "Deprecated where() would fail on first query" conflates deprecation with failure. The pinned google-cloud-firestore>=2.16.0,<3.0.0 still SUPPORTS positional where() — it emits a DeprecationWarning, it does not error. The real failure cause is the missing composite index, not the deprecated where(). The finding bundles these as co-equal failure causes; only the index one is a hard failure.
2. "Promise undelivered / entire UI broken in prod" — the limitation is openly disclosed, not hidden. README:16-19 states the cloud paths (incl. Firestore) are "wired and unit-tested but not yet validated against live GCP". docs/ADK-GUIDE.md:158 carries an explicit "Caveat — not validated against live Firestore: composite indexes, FieldFilter migration, and is_flagged backfill are pre-deploy steps gated by the skipped test_firestore_emulator.py". The adapter docstring and the emulator test's pre-deploy checklist (lines 6-19) enumerate exactly these steps. So this is a known, gated, documented pre-deploy task — not a silent landmine that contradicts the docs.
3. Not "any real query" fails: existing_ids (single doc.get), get_run/create_run (doc-level), list_runs (single order_by, no filter) work without composite indexes; get_items_for_run uses two equality filters (auto-indexed). Only the filtered list_news path is the realistic break.

Net: the Firestore backend is genuinely unproven against a real store and a config-swap-to-prod would hit a missing-index FAILED_PRECONDITION on filtered news queries — a real incompleteness gap. But it is honestly labeled as such across README, ADK-GUIDE, the adapter docstring, and a deliberately-skipped emulator gate. That makes it a documented pre-deploy TODO, not a high-severity broken promise. Severity corrected high -> medium.

title: Firestore existing_ids does N individual document gets — O(N) round-trips for dedup
evidence: firestore_backend.py:40-44: existing_ids returns '{i for i in ids if col.document(i).get().exists}' — one synchronous .get() round-trip per candidate id. The SQLite equivalent (sqlite_backend.py:65-73) does a SINGLE batched 'SELECT id ... WHERE id IN (placeholders)'. The dedup stage (NormalizeDedup) calls existing_ids with the full batch of newly collected items, so on Firestore a single digest run collecting K items issues K sequential network gets.
impact: The two adapters have the same contract but wildly different cost/latency profiles for the hot dedup path. On Firestore this is O(N) network round-trips per run (slow, and billed per read), versus O(1) queries on SQLite. Functionally correct on the fake, operationally poor on real Firestore — another reason the swap is not production-ready as written.
recommendation: Batch the existence check: use Firestore get_all([refs]) (one call) or chunked 'where(__name__, in, ids)' queries (10/30-id limit per chunk). This is a focused fix that should land with the broader Firestore-hardening work, or be explicitly deferred if Firestore is marked experimental.
verifier_notes:
Confirmed exactly as reported. firestore_backend.py:40-44: existing_ids returns `{i for i in ids if col.document(i).get().exists}` — one synchronous .get() per candidate id = O(N) sequential network round-trips, and N billed reads on real Firestore. sqlite_backend.py:65-73: single batched `SELECT id FROM news_items WHERE id IN (placeholders)` (id is PRIMARY KEY, indexed) = O(1) query. normalize.py:43 (NormalizeDedup) calls existing_ids with the full batch of K candidates, so a Firestore run issues K sequential gets. Both adapters share an identical port contract (storage.py:15), so the divergent cost/latency is real, not a contract mismatch.

Additional aggravating detail the auditor did not mention: youtube.py:263 calls existing_ids([item_id]) INSIDE a per-video loop, so the YouTube collector also issues one Firestore .get() per video sequentially — same O(N) pattern via a second call path.

Two caveats on severity (not refutations): (1) The Firestore adapter is correctness-preserving — existing_ids returns the right set; this is a performance/cost issue, not strictly data-integrity. (2) firestore_backend.py's own header (lines 1-12) flags the adapter as unvalidated against real/emulated Firestore and SQLite is the v1 live backend, with Firestore behind an optional extra. So the impact is latent until Firestore is actually deployed. Medium is reasonable for a hot-path O(N)-reads pattern that becomes a real cost/latency blocker once the prod backend is enabled; it sits near the medium/low boundary because it is not yet on the live path and is functionally correct.

title: Durable-storage code is plumbed only into the local path — the deployed container uses in-memory sessions AND ephemeral SQLite
evidence: The Dockerfile CMD (line 39) runs 'uvicorn app.fast_api_app:app'. fast_api_app.py:57-58 hardcodes 'session_service_uri = None' (comment: 'In-memory session configuration - no persistent storage') and passes it to get_fast_api_app at :67 — so the deployed ADK surface NEVER uses the durable DatabaseSessionService that runner.make_session_service (runner.py:74-78) defaults to. Meanwhile the product /api/* routes registered into the same app (fast_api_app.py:77) call run_digest -> build_storage -> SqliteBackend(settings.sqlite_path), whose default (config.py:82) is REPO_ROOT/data/catchup.db i.e. /code/data inside the container — Cloud Run's ephemeral, per-instance, wiped-on-restart filesystem. The Dockerfile (COPY at lines 23,27) ships only app/ and config/, never declares a VOLUME, and never sets STORAGE_BACKEND=firestore.
impact: Both durable-storage stories the docs advertise (persistent ADK sessions for resumable runs; Firestore/SQLite for the product DB) are DEAD on the actual deploy artifact. A deployed digest run would write its DigestRun/NewsItem rows to a disk that vanishes on the next cold start or scale-to-zero, and ADK session state is never persisted at all. This is the concrete, code-level core of the 'agents and gcp webapp and sqlite all scattered' complaint: the well-tested durable layer exists but is wired exclusively to the local run_digest/serve path, not the container the prod docs point at.
recommendation: Reconcile the deploy surface with the durable layer: (a) set session_service_uri from settings (DatabaseSessionService via Cloud SQL, or at minimum honor session_db_url) instead of hardcoding None; (b) force STORAGE_BACKEND=firestore (once Firestore is actually hardened per the first finding) in the deployed env so the product DB is durable, OR mount a persistent volume / Cloud SQL for SQLite; (c) if neither is funded, downgrade the README/ARCHITECTURE prod claims to local-only and stop presenting the container as a durable deployment. The current state is durable-in-code, ephemeral-in-deploy.
verifier_notes:
Code-level facts all verify. Dockerfile:39 CMD = `uvicorn app.fast_api_app:app`. fast_api_app.py:57-58 hardcodes `session_service_uri = None` ("In-memory session configuration") and passes it to get_fast_api_app at :67, so the deployed ADK surface uses in-memory sessions, NOT the DatabaseSessionService that runner.py:74-78 (make_session_service) defaults to. The same app registers /api/* (fast_api_app.py:77); POST /api/runs -> run_trigger.try_start_run -> run_digest -> build_storage -> SqliteBackend(settings.sqlite_path) (api/app.py:278, run_trigger.py, runner.py:52-62). config.py:81-82 defaults storage_backend="sqlite" and sqlite_path=REPO_ROOT/data/catchup.db (=/code/data in-container). Dockerfile COPYs only app/ + config/, no VOLUME, no STORAGE_BACKEND=firestore; /data/ is gitignored so the DB dir is created at runtime on the ephemeral FS. There is NO Terraform/Cloud Build/deployment dir anywhere in the repo to inject firestore or a persistent volume — only the Dockerfile exists.

Why PARTIAL, not confirmed at "high": the IMPACT framing is overstated. (1) There is no prod deploy artifact "the prod docs point at" — docs/ADK-GUIDE.md:165 explicitly states the milestone target is local/self-hosted and the Agent Engine/Cloud Run path is FUTURE. So the data-loss scenario is a hypothetical readiness gap, not an active production bug. (2) The in-memory ADK session claim is technically true but functionally near-zero impact: product data (DigestRun/NewsItem) is persisted by run_digest/build_storage to SQLite, NOT to the ADK session. The ADK session only carries transient state_delta within one synchronous run_async inside a single run_digest call; the session is created fresh per run and never reloaded across processes in this fire-and-forget daemon-thread flow, so memory-vs-database ADK sessions makes no behavioral difference here. The "resumable runs" benefit is not consumed anywhere. The real, genuine residual issue is the narrower one: if the Dockerfile image is run on Cloud Run as-is, digest rows land on ephemeral per-instance disk (no volume, sqlite default) and vanish on restart/scale-to-zero — a real deployment-readiness gap, but a documented local-milestone limitation rather than a high-severity production data-loss regression. Corrected severity: medium.

title: Spec's "structured-output validation with one retry" is unmet — validation failures are never retried and drop the whole batch, not the item
evidence: Spec §13 line 193: "Structured-output validation with one retry; on failure item stays raw". Actual flow: adk_enrich (processing.py:62-66) calls run_agent_text(...) THEN parse_model_json(text, ProcessingResult). run_agent_text (runtime.py:83-109) retries `attempts = 1 + max(0, settings.llm_max_retries)` (default llm_max_retries=2, i.e. 3 attempts) but only inside its try around `_run_coro_sync(_run_text_async(...))` — parse_model_json runs AFTER it returns, OUTSIDE the retry loop. So a malformed-JSON / schema-mismatch (the actual "structured-output validation" failure) raises LLMOutputError and is NOT retried at all. It propagates to process_items (processing.py:155-159) which catches per BATCH and `continue`s, so EVERY item in that batch falls to status="raw" (processing.py:164-166), not just the offending item. The retry that DOES exist is a transport retry on transient runner/timeout Exceptions, not validation, and its count is 2 not the spec's "one".
impact: A single transient model hiccup that yields slightly-off JSON discards up to llm_batch_size (default 8) enriched items per batch with no second attempt, and the resilience doc overstates a guarantee the code does not provide. Wastes the LLM call (no re-prompt) and silently degrades digest coverage. The mismatch between the documented guarantee and behavior is part of what makes the project feel "not behaving the way it's supposed to".
recommendation: Either (a) wrap parse_model_json INSIDE the retry envelope so a validation failure triggers exactly one re-prompt (matching the spec), and shrink the batch on validation failure so only the failing item — not the whole batch — drops to raw; or (b) downgrade the spec line to describe the actual behavior (transport retry only; batch-granular raw fallthrough). Also reconcile llm_max_retries default (2) with the spec's "one".
verifier_notes:
All technical claims verified against the code.

FLOW: adk_enrich (app/pipeline/processing.py:62-66) calls run_agent_text(...) then parse_model_json(text, ProcessingResult). run_agent_text (app/llm/runtime.py:83-109) wraps ONLY _run_coro_sync(_run_text_async(...)) in its retry loop (lines 96-107). parse_model_json executes back in adk_enrich AFTER run_agent_text returns — outside the retry loop. So a schema/JSON-validation failure (LLMOutputError, raised in app/llm/parse.py:88-111, a ValueError subclass) is NOT retried. Confirmed.

RETRY IS TRANSPORT-ONLY: the except Exception (runtime.py:100) catches transient runner/timeout failures from the LLM call itself; a successful call that returns slightly-off JSON returns normally (no exception inside the loop), so it is never re-prompted. Confirmed.

COUNT: attempts = 1 + max(0, settings.llm_max_retries); config.py:95 sets llm_max_retries=2 → 3 attempts, i.e. 2 retries, not the spec's "one". Confirmed.

BATCH GRANULARITY: process_items (processing.py:154-159) wraps enrich(batch) in try/except per BATCH; on failure it appends an error dict and continues. Items absent from `enrichments` fall to status="raw" (processing.py:162-166). Since the whole batch failed, none of its items are in `enrichments`, so EVERY item in that batch (up to llm_batch_size=8, config.py:86) goes to raw — not just the offending item. Confirmed. No higher-level retry wraps process_items (agents.py:284-294 calls it once; batch errors become source_errors). The same run_agent_text-then-parse_model_json anti-pattern repeats in critic.py, judge.py, digest_editor.py, and adk_reprocess.

SPEC: docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-23-adk-catchup-agent-design.md §13 line 193 says "Structured-output validation with one retry; on failure item stays `raw` (never crashes the run)."

WHY MEDIUM not HIGH: (1) The core spec guarantee — never crashes the run, failed items stay raw — IS honored; the raw-fallback works (it's just batch-granular, not item-granular). The finding's phrasing "drop the whole batch, not the item" slightly overstates by implying the raw-fallback is broken; it isn't, it's coarse. (2) The unmet parts are the "one retry" on validation and item-level granularity — a resilience/quality degradation, not a correctness/security defect; coverage recovers next run per the documented graceful-degradation design. (3) The processing agent is built with output_schema=ProcessingResult (processing.py:56), which schema-constrains Gemini's output and makes validation failures relatively rare in practice, bounding real-world impact. Genuine contract-drift, real but bounded impact — medium.

title: YouTube summarizer bypasses the hardened parse_model_json, re-introducing the exact JSON fragility the LLM layer eliminates
evidence: adk_summarize (youtube.py:205-211) calls `raw = run_agent_text(agent, text, settings)` then `return DigestNarrative.model_validate_json(raw).narrative` — a raw Pydantic JSON parse. Every other LLM stage (processing.py:66, critic.py:71, digest_editor.py:48, judge.py:70) routes the model text through app.llm.parse.parse_model_json, which strips Markdown code fences and extracts the first balanced JSON value (parse.py:28-111). model_validate_json does none of that, so a fenced or prose-wrapped response (```json ... ``` or "Here is the summary: {...}") raises a hard ValidationError instead of being repaired.
impact: YouTube summaries are the only LLM output that crashes on the common code-fence/prose noise the rest of the pipeline tolerates. The failure isn't isolated like processing batches — it raises inside the youtube collector's summarize boundary, producing a source_error and dropping that item, inconsistently with every other stage.
recommendation: Replace `DigestNarrative.model_validate_json(raw).narrative` with `parse_model_json(raw, DigestNarrative).narrative` (import from app.llm.parse) so YouTube uses the same hardened repair path as the rest of the pipeline.
verifier_notes:
CONFIRMED. The code matches the finding exactly. youtube.py:205-211 (adk_summarize) does `raw = run_agent_text(agent, text, settings)` then `return DigestNarrative.model_validate_json(raw).narrative` — a raw Pydantic parse with no repair. grep over app/ confirms every other LLM stage routes through app.llm.parse.parse_model_json: processing.py:66 and :114, critic.py:71, digest_editor.py:48, judge.py:70. parse.py:88-111 does the repair (_strip_code_fences at 28-42, _extract_first_json_value at 45-85); raw model_validate_json does none of it, so a fenced/prose-wrapped response raises ValidationError instead of being repaired.

Strongest corroboration: digest_editor.py:41-48 (adk_narrate) is a near-identical twin of adk_summarize — same DigestNarrative output_schema, same output_key, same run_agent_text bridge — yet it hardens with parse_model_json while youtube.py does not. This shows the protection was deliberately applied elsewhere and simply omitted in youtube.py, not a design choice. (The sqlite_backend.py model_validate_json calls are trusted DB rows, correctly excluded.)

Impact verified and, if anything, understated: youtube.collect (youtube.py:259-282) has NO per-video try/except, so a ValidationError on one video aborts the entire collect() and propagates to the collector wrapper at agents.py:206-216, which catches it into run.source_errors keyed by source_id. So the blast radius is the whole channel's batch (every remaining video for that source dropped), not a single item as the finding states.

Severity kept at medium rather than raised, because of one real mitigation the finding omits: the agent is built with output_schema=DigestNarrative (youtube.py:200), configuring Gemini/ADK for controlled structured JSON output, which makes code-fence/prose noise materially less likely than for free-form text — lowering crash probability. But since the maintainers still chose to harden the structurally-identical digest_editor (also output_schema-constrained) with parse_model_json, the inconsistency and the whole-source blast radius justify medium. Honest range: low-to-medium; medium is defensible. One-line fix: route youtube.py:211 through parse_model_json like every sibling stage.

title: Runtime narration is unbounded — the top-12 ranker is dead; DigestEditor sends every rendered item to the LLM in one call
evidence: write_narrative(items, generate, top_n=12) (digest_editor.py:23-27) sorts by importance_score and caps to the top 12 before generating. grep shows its only callers are tests (tests/unit/test_digest_editor.py); it is NEVER called on the runtime path. The runtime path is DigestEditorAgent.narrator = _default_narrator = `adk_narrate(rendered, settings)` (runner.py:99-100; agents.py:400), where `rendered = select_rendered(items)` (runner.py:111-113) returns ALL processed items (or ALL non-flagged if none processed) with no cap. adk_narrate (digest_editor.py:41-48) JSON-serializes every one of those items' title/summary/category into a single LLM call.
impact: On a large run (many enabled sources) the digest editor receives an unbounded payload — higher token cost, higher latency, and risk of exceeding the model context, with no prioritization of what matters most. The intended top-12 "what matters most" curation exists in code and tests but never runs in production. This is exactly the kind of design-vs-reality gap the user senses.
recommendation: Wire write_narrative (or an equivalent top_n cap) into adk_narrate / the DigestEditorAgent so the narrative is built from the top-N items by importance, OR delete write_narrative and its tests if unbounded narration is intentional. Make the runtime match the documented "what matters most" curation.
verifier_notes:
Finding is accurate on every concrete claim. write_narrative(items, generate, top_n=12) at app/pipeline/digest_editor.py:23-27 sorts by importance_score and caps to the top 12 before generating. A whole-repo grep for write_narrative shows its ONLY callers are tests (tests/unit/test_digest_editor.py:18,24); nothing in app/ calls it. The runtime path does NOT use it: build_pipeline sets `_narrator = narrator or _default_narrator(settings)` (agents.py:476) and passes it to DigestEditorAgent(narrator=_narrator) (agents.py:531-535). _default_narrator returns `lambda items: digest_editor.adk_narrate(items, settings)` (runner.py:99-100). In DigestEditorAgent._run_async_impl, `rendered = select_rendered(items)` (agents.py:395) then `self.narrator(rendered)` (agents.py:400). select_rendered (runner.py:111-113) returns all processed items, or all non-flagged if none processed — NO cap, no importance sort. adk_narrate (digest_editor.py:41-48) json.dumps title/summary_en/category for EVERY item into one run_agent_text LLM call. So the top-12 ranker is genuinely dead code, exercised only by tests, while production narration is uncapped. Dead-code claim fully confirmed.

One mild correction to the IMPACT framing, not the verdict: payload is not strictly unbounded in all cases — API/newsapi sources cap at max_articles=10 per source (app/services/newsapi.py:20,24). However RSS iterates over ALL parsed.entries with no cap (app/services/rss.py:49) and there is NO global item cap anywhere upstream, so total items still scale with the number of enabled sources and feed sizes. The 'no prioritization of what matters most at runtime' and 'cost/latency grow with sources' points are correct. For a personal news-catchup tool the practical context-overflow risk is moderate rather than severe, so medium severity is appropriate and I am leaving it unchanged.

title: Digest output-map keys mismatch: Markdown & Excel links never render in the UI
evidence: Backend RenderAgent writes the run.outputs dict with keys 'md', 'xlsx', 'html' (app/pipeline/agents.py:438-440): run.outputs["md"]=..., run.outputs["xlsx"]=..., run.outputs["html"]=.... The frontend OutputLinks component expects keys 'html', 'excel', 'markdown' (output-links.tsx:8-10 OUTPUT_ORDER=["html","excel","markdown"]) and renders only entries where `key in outputs` (output-links.tsx:19). run-detail.tsx:108 passes run.outputs through verbatim with no remapping; a repo-wide grep found NO md->markdown / xlsx->excel translation anywhere in frontend src. So only 'html' intersects; 'md' and 'xlsx' are produced on disk but silently dropped by the UI filter.
impact: Two of the three digest deliverables (Markdown and Excel) are generated every run but are invisible in the console — the user sees only the HTML entry and may conclude the Markdown/Excel renderers are broken or missing, reinforcing the 'features not behaving as intended' complaint. It is a silent failure: no error, no console warning, just missing rows.
recommendation: Pick ONE canonical key set and enforce it. Cheapest fix: change the backend keys to match the frontend ('markdown'/'excel'/'html') in app/pipeline/agents.py:438-439, OR change the frontend OUTPUT_ORDER/OUTPUT_META keys to 'md'/'xlsx'/'html' in output-links.tsx:8-16. Add a tiny test asserting the key set so the two sides can't drift again (the project already has test_markdown/test_excel/test_html — extend one to assert the run.outputs keys).
verifier_notes:
The core claim is fully verified against the real code. Backend app/pipeline/agents.py:438-440 writes run.outputs with keys "md", "xlsx", "html". run.outputs is a free-form dict[str,str] (app/core/domain.py:183) and app/cli.py:23 confirms "md" is the actual key the backend uses. The frontend never remaps: lib/schemas.ts:69 validates outputs as z.record(z.string(), z.string()) (accepts any keys, no transformation); run-detail.tsx:108 passes run.outputs verbatim to OutputLinks. output-links.tsx:10 defines OUTPUT_ORDER = ["html","excel","markdown"] and line 19 filters OUTPUT_ORDER.filter(key => key in outputs). Only "html" intersects; "md"/"xlsx" are dropped silently (no error, no warning). A repo-wide grep confirms no md->markdown / xlsx->excel translation exists anywhere in frontend source (all other markdown/excel hits are node_modules noise). So 2 of 3 deliverables are invisible in the console — the finding holds.

Severity correction high->medium: two nuances temper the impact. (1) These are NOT clickable links/downloads — OutputLinks renders informational filename badges (basename text only) and the component explicitly states "Output files are written to the API host filesystem (not served over the web)." So nothing is fetchable through the UI regardless; the bug is missing display rows, not broken downloads. (2) The files ARE generated on disk and the backend works correctly — no data loss, no exception, no functional breakage of the deliverables. It is a cosmetic/contract-drift display defect with genuine but limited user-visible impact, fixable with a one-line key alignment. Medium fits better than high.

title: Static-export console's same-origin base URL holds for desktop but is false on Cloud Run (no frontend deployed)
evidence: api.ts:46-48 (and the duplicated logic in health-pill.tsx:9-11) resolve the API base to '' (same-origin) when NODE_ENV==='production'. That is correct ONLY for the desktop single-port app, where create_app() calls mount_console(app, settings.console_dir) (app/api/app.py:366-367) so the FastAPI process serves both / and /api on one port. The Cloud Run path uses a DIFFERENT entrypoint: Dockerfile CMD runs app.fast_api_app:app on 8080 (Dockerfile:38) and only COPYs ./app and ./config (Dockerfile:23,27) — it never runs `next build` nor copies frontend/out. fast_api_app.py builds the ADK get_fast_api_app surface and calls register_product_routes() (fast_api_app.py:62-77) but never calls mount_console. So the deployed container serves /api/* and the ADK web UI but ships NO console. There is no environment in which a browser loads the static console from the Cloud Run origin, so the production same-origin '' base resolves against an origin that has no frontend.
impact: The 'frontend on Vercel/Cloud Run, same-origin in prod' story is only half-wired. If the console were hosted separately (e.g. Vercel) it would resolve base='' and call ITS OWN origin (no /api there) unless NEXT_PUBLIC_API_BASE is set — and nothing in the build sets it for that case. This is the concrete contract face of the user's 'gcp webapp and agents are scattered': the deployed product API and the deployed UI never coexist on one origin the way desktop does.
recommendation: Decide the prod topology and make the contract match it. Either (a) have the Dockerfile build the Next.js export and have fast_api_app.py call mount_console so Cloud Run is genuinely single-port same-origin like desktop, or (b) explicitly document that prod requires NEXT_PUBLIC_API_BASE pointing at the Cloud Run API origin and ship a CORS allowlist + build-time env wiring for the separately-hosted console. Until one is chosen, mark the Cloud Run path as API-only in README so the same-origin assumption isn't presented as production-ready.
verifier_notes:
Verified all four cited files plus app/api/app.py and config.py.

CONFIRMED facts:
- api.ts:46-48 and health-pill.tsx:9-11 resolve base to '' (same-origin) when NODE_ENV==='production' and NEXT_PUBLIC_API_BASE is unset. Accurate.
- mount_console is called ONLY by create_app() in app/api/app.py:366-367, guarded by `if Path(settings.console_dir).is_dir()`. settings.console_dir defaults to frontend/out (config.py:153). next.config.ts uses output:"export" emitting out/.
- Dockerfile copies only ./app (line 23) and ./config (line 27); it never runs `next build` nor copies frontend/out. CMD runs app.fast_api_app:app on 8080 (line 37-38).
- fast_api_app.py does NOT call mount_console; it builds the ADK get_fast_api_app surface (web=True) and calls register_product_routes(app, _settings) at lines 73-77.
- Net: the Cloud Run container serves /api/* and the ADK web UI but ships no static console. The finding's "no environment loads the static console from the Cloud Run origin" is correct for the deployed image. The same-origin '' base is correct for the desktop single-port app (the documented primary path).

WHY PARTIAL / OVERSTATED:
- The finding's framing that the deployed container "ships NO console" is literally true, but its impact narrative ("the deployed product API and the deployed UI never coexist") understates that register_product_routes IS deployed on Cloud Run specifically so an externally-hosted console can reach a real /api backend — fast_api_app.py:73-74 documents this intent explicitly. The split is deliberate and documented in the module docstring (lines 24-28), not an accidental half-wiring.
- No currently-supported deployment path is broken: desktop = console+API same origin (works); Cloud Run = API + ADK UI for an external console that must set NEXT_PUBLIC_API_BASE (api.ts:45-48 comment notes an explicit base, incl "", always wins). The real gap is that NOTHING in the build sets NEXT_PUBLIC_API_BASE for a separately-hosted (e.g. Vercel) console — a deployment-completeness/documentation gap for a not-yet-built hosting target, not a runtime bug in shipped code.
- Severity downgraded high -> medium: real, factually accurate about the build/entrypoint, but it describes an unfinished future hosting story rather than a broken contract in any shipped/supported deployment.

title: Categories screen (UI screen 5) missing and taxonomy is hardcoded, not configurable (FR1/FR7 gap)
evidence: Spec §2 goal: 'Cover 4 default categories ... (configurable taxonomy)' and §17 screen 5: 'Categories — manage taxonomy.' Reality: domain.py:21-25 Category is a fixed StrEnum (ai_tech, business_finance, world_geopolitics, gulf_mena) — adding/renaming a category requires a code change + redeploy, not config. There is no Categories page (frontend/app has no categories/ dir) and no categories CRUD endpoint (grep of app/api/app.py shows Category used only as a read-only list_news filter at app.py:248 and a dashboard count at 223-224). The only category UI is the read-only CategoryBreakdown dashboard widget.

recommendation: Decide explicitly: (a) downgrade the spec to '4 fixed categories' and drop screen 5 + the 'configurable taxonomy' claim, or (b) move the taxonomy to config/categories.yaml with a CRUD endpoint + screen. Current state silently disagrees with the spec.
verifier_notes:
Every factual claim in the finding holds up against the code and spec.

BACKEND (app/core/domain.py:21-25): Category is a fixed StrEnum with exactly 4 members (ai_tech, business_finance, world_geopolitics, gulf_mena). Adding/renaming a category requires a code change. There is NO config/categories.yaml (config/ only has sources.yaml and watchlist.yaml — both of which DO have CRUD endpoints, unlike categories).

API (app/api/app.py): Category is imported (line 33) and used only as (a) a read-only filter param in GET /news (line 248) and (b) a dashboard count aggregation (lines 223-224, 228). The only PUT/POST/DELETE routes are /settings, /sources, /watchlist, /runs, /sources/resolve. There is NO categories CRUD endpoint, despite the spec's API surface listing "CRUD /api/categories" (illustrative, line 247).

FRONTEND: frontend/app/ has digests/, news/, settings/, sources/, watchlist/ subdirs — NO categories/ dir, so screen 5 is absent. No nav/sidebar link to categories. The taxonomy is hardcoded in two more places: frontend/lib/types.ts:2 (Category union of the same 4 strings) and frontend/lib/categories.ts (CATEGORY_COLORS keyed on the 4 values). The only category UI is the read-only CategoryBreakdown widget (frontend/components/dashboard/category-breakdown.tsx), which itself hardcodes CATEGORY_ORDER (lines 5-10).

SPEC claims all verified verbatim in docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-23-adk-catchup-agent-design.md: §2 goal line 22 "(configurable taxonomy)"; §17 IA line 237 "5. Categories — manage taxonomy."; FR1 (line 40) category hint and FR7 (line 46) "categories config"; and notably §6 line 96 "Configuration over code: sources, categories, watchlist, schedule, provider all declarative" — categories are EXPLICITLY named as a config-over-code item, so the gap directly contradicts the stated design principle.

SEVERITY CORRECTION (high -> medium): The finding is real and well-evidenced as a scope gap, but "high" overstates it. This is a missing feature (1 of 8 planned screens) plus a missing declarative-config affordance, not a correctness/security/data-loss defect. The 4 categories work end-to-end (classification, filtering, dashboard). There is a clear (if inconvenient) workaround — edit the enum + TS union + colors map and redeploy. For a v1 single-user local desktop app, this is a contained limitation. Medium is the appropriate severity for an unbuilt config screen that contradicts a stated principle but causes no runtime breakage.

title: No schedule UI or API — FR9 'configurable schedule' is config-file-only; Runs & Schedule screen (7) has no scheduling half
evidence: The scheduler is real and wired (scheduler.py build_scheduler reads settings.schedule_enabled/schedule_cron/schedule_timezone; started in app.py lifespan 339-344). (6) Run-now half IS delivered: run-now-button.tsx -> api.triggerRun() -> POST /api/runs (app.py:273-281). Accurate.

title: FR6 'expose outputs' unmet — Excel/HTML/Markdown are rendered to disk but cannot be opened or downloaded from the console
evidence: FR6: 'Render Excel, HTML dashboard, and Markdown outputs; persist + expose them.' Render stage writes all three (agents.py:438-440 markdown.write_markdown / excel.write_excel / html_render.write_html into settings.output_dir) and records paths in run.outputs. But the UI only LISTS the filenames and explicitly states they are not reachable: output-links.tsx renders the basename as plain text and a caption 'Output files are written to the API host filesystem (not served over the web).' impact: The 'beautiful HTML dashboard' / Excel / Markdown deliverables — a headline FR — are produced but a console user cannot view or download them. On a Cloud Run deploy the output_dir is ephemeral too, so the files are effectively unreachable.
recommendation: Add an authenticated GET /api/runs/{id}/output/{kind} (FileResponse with the recorded path, path-validated against output_dir) and make output-links.tsx render real download/open links. Without this, FR6 should be marked PARTIAL in the docs.
verifier_notes:
All four evidence points hold up against the real code.

1. Render writes all three outputs: app/pipeline/agents.py:438-440 calls markdown.write_markdown / excel.write_excel / html_render.write_html into self.settings.output_dir (config.py:84 -> REPO_ROOT/output) and stores paths in run.outputs. Confirmed.

2. UI only lists basenames as PLAIN TEXT: output-links.tsx:29 `const basename = path.split("/").pop()` rendered in a <span className="font-mono"> (line 38). grep for <a/href/download/<Link returned NONE — there is no clickable link or download affordance. The caption at lines 43-46 reads verbatim: "Output files are written to the API host filesystem (not served over the web)." Quote is exact.

4. FR6 spec text confirmed verbatim at docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-23-adk-catchup-agent-design.md:45: "Render Excel, HTML dashboard, and Markdown outputs; persist + expose them." The persist half is met (files written, paths recorded, listed in UI); the expose half is not (unreachable from console).

OutputLinks is the genuine UI surface (used in run-detail.tsx:108 as the run header action; runs-list.tsx only shows a count of run.outputs keys).

Severity stays medium (not raised/lowered): it is a real scope gap on a headline FR, and the Cloud Run ephemeral-output impact is also accurate. It is partial-feature non-compliance rather than a security/data-loss defect, so medium — not high — is correct. Marked confirmed (not partial) because the auditor did not overstate: every specific claim is literally true in the code.

title: FR10 swap-by-config is half-surfaced: Vertex toggle and scheduler swap have no user-facing control; Firestore is unproven
evidence: FR10: 'Switch LLM provider, storage, and scheduler via configuration only.' Code level: storage swap is real (StorageBackend port + sqlite/firestore adapters selected by Settings.storage_backend via build_storage). Vertex swap is real in configure_genai (runtime.py reads settings.use_vertexai). BUT: (1) nothing in the UI/managed config writes use_vertexai — PUT /api/settings (app.py:182-214) only handles google_api_key + app_port, so 'free local -> Vertex prod' needs manual dotenv edits; (2) the scheduler has NO port/adapter — it is a flat 36-line APScheduler function (scheduler.py) with no Cloud Scheduler seam, so 'switch the scheduler by config' has nothing to switch against; (3) firestore_backend.py is contract-green only against an in-memory fake and the emulator test is a permanently-skipped stub, so the storage swap is aspirational against a real store.
impact: The 'no business logic differs between v1 and prod / swap by config only' promise (§12) is delivered for storage selection in code, but operationally a user cannot flip provider/schedule from the product, and the Firestore option would fail on first real query. This is a core driver of the user's 'agents + gcp webapp + sqlite all scattered' feeling.
recommendation: Surface use_vertexai (and storage_backend) in PUT /api/settings; introduce a scheduler port + cloud_scheduler adapter to give FR10's scheduler clause a real seam; either fund Firestore (emulator test, FieldFilter, indexes) or mark it experimental in the README so the FR10 claim matches reality.
verifier_notes:
All three sub-claims verified against source; if anything the finding understates the scheduler gap.

CONTRACT IS REAL: docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-23-adk-catchup-agent-design.md line 49 = FR10 "Switch LLM provider, storage, and scheduler via configuration only."; line 185 (§12) = "A Settings-driven factory wires the concrete adapters. No business logic differs between v1 and prod." Line 179 explicitly promises core/ports/scheduler.py (Scheduler ABC: schedule(job,cron), trigger_now()) plus adapters/scheduling/local_scheduler.py | cloud_scheduler.py.

(1) NO UI/managed-config control for Vertex/storage/schedule — CONFIRMED. app/api/app.py: SettingsPatch (lines 81-85) and put_settings (lines 182-214) only handle google_api_key + app_port. app/core/config.py:35 _MANAGED_ENV_KEYS = ("GOOGLE_API_KEY","APP_PORT"). grep finds no write path for use_vertexai / storage_backend / schedule_* anywhere in app/api, app/services, or env_store. configure_genai (runtime.py:43) does read settings.use_vertexai, and build_storage (runner.py:52-62) does select sqlite/firestore by settings.storage_backend — so the code-level swaps are real, but flipping free-local -> Vertex prod requires hand-editing a dotenv file.

(2) Scheduler has NO port/adapter — CONFIRMED AND STRONGER THAN CLAIMED. app/core/ports/ contains only __init__.py + storage.py (no scheduler.py). app/adapters/ contains only storage/ (no scheduling/, no local_scheduler.py / cloud_scheduler.py). The sole scheduler is the flat 36-line app/services/scheduler.py build_scheduler() wrapping APScheduler with no seam; the "Cloud Scheduler" path is just a docs note (config.py:119) to point Cloud Scheduler at POST /api/runs, not a config-selectable adapter. The promised Scheduler ABC + two adapters from the design doc simply do not exist, so "switch the scheduler via config" has nothing to switch.

(3) Firestore unproven — CONFIRMED. tests/integration/test_firestore_emulator.py is pytestmark = pytest.mark.skip(...) and its body raises AssertionError("implement against the emulator before deploy"); offline coverage is only against an in-memory fake. The adapter's own docstring (firestore_backend.py lines 6-8) and the test checklist (lines 9-13) state positional where(field,op,value) is deprecated by real Firestore (needs FieldFilter), composite indexes are missing, and is_flagged backfill is required — i.e. authors themselves flag it would break on a real store. The "fail on first real query" claim is provider-version-dependent (slightly strong) but author-documented.

Severity medium is correct: the shipped default path (sqlite + AI Studio) works; this is documented-contract vs implementation drift with operational impact on the prod-swap story, not a crash in the default product. Category contract-drift is accurate.
