title: SSRF guard enforces scheme and private-IP block and timeout but has no response-size cap
evidence: safe_get (net.py:74-132) sets a timeout and reads the full body via client.send; callers use resp.text/content (rss.py:16, scrape.py:17, youtube.py:54, newsapi.py:32, feed_discovery.py:16, youtube_resolve.py:22). No Content-Length check, no streaming byte ceiling, no oversized-body abort. feedparser and BeautifulSoup parse the entire payload in memory. The promised SSRF size limit is missing; only time exists.
impact: A user-supplied source URL (PUT sources/resolve) at a public endpoint streaming a huge or endless body within the timeout can exhaust memory and OOM-kill the process. IP-pin/scheme do not help since the target is a legitimate public host. A DoS amplification vector on a user-controlled, server-fetched input.
recommendation: Stream the response in safe_get and raise UnsafeURLError past a configurable max (5-10 MB); reject when Content-Length exceeds the cap.
verifier_notes:
Core claim verified against the real code. app/services/net.py safe_get (lines 74-132) enforces scheme allow-list + private-IP block + IP-pinning + per-hop redirect re-validation + a 15s timeout, but has NO response-size cap: line 125 does a non-streamed resp = client.send(request) (no stream=True, no httpx.Limits, no max_size, no iter_bytes ceiling, no Content-Length check). Confirmed every caller consumes the full body into memory: rss.py:18 resp.content, scrape.py:19 resp.text, feed_discovery.py:18 resp.content, youtube.py:56 resp.content, newsapi.py:34 resp.json(), youtube_resolve.py:24 resp.content. feedparser/BeautifulSoup then parse the whole payload.

Reachability confirmed: app/api/app.py:283 POST /sources/resolve (resolve_source) passes the user-supplied body.url straight into youtube_resolve.resolve_channel_id / feed_discovery.discover_feed, both of which call safe_get. When settings.api_key is unset (the default local/dev path), _require_api_key is a no-op and the API is fully OPEN — app.py:151-155 even logs a warning to that effect — so this is unauthenticated in an unkeyed deployment. The route is gated by require_api_key + rate_limit (TokenBucket), but the rate limiter only throttles request frequency; it does nothing to bound a single response body. The 15s timeout caps slow/endless streams but does NOT cap a fast, large body (e.g. multi-GB file from a legitimate public host) which lands entirely in memory -> OOM. IP-pin/scheme guards do not mitigate this since the target is a real public host.

Two minor labeling inaccuracies in the finding (do not refute it): the route is POST /sources/resolve, not 'PUT sources/resolve' (PUT /sources at app.py:259 is a separate endpoint); and it is 'public/unauthenticated' only when API_KEY is unset, otherwise it requires the key. Severity medium is correct: real, exploitable memory-exhaustion gap on server-side fetch of user-controlled input, but bounded by the timeout (slow streams), the rate limiter (frequency), and auth in any keyed deployment.

title: Rate limiting is a single global bucket on two routes only, not per-client and not on collectors
evidence: TokenBucket (ratelimit.py:8-37) is a single shared bucket with no per-IP/key dimension. app.py:144-149 apply it only to POST runs (273) and POST sources resolve (284); read routes and PUT sources/watchlist have none. Collectors call safe_get unthrottled, so spec per-source rate limiting is absent.
impact: One client can drain the bucket and deny POST runs/resolve to everyone (cross-tenant DoS); read/PUT routes are unthrottled. Per-source rate-limit promise undelivered. OK for single-user desktop, inadequate for the advertised Cloud Run deploy.
recommendation: If exposed beyond loopback, key the limiter per client (api key/IP) and apply to all write/expensive routes; add a per-source throttle or downgrade the docs claim.
verifier_notes:
Every factual claim holds against the code. (1) ratelimit.py:8-37 TokenBucket has NO per-IP/key dimension; app.py:114-121 _rate_limiter closes over ONE shared rate_bucket built once at app.py:144-147 (docstring literally says "shared per-process token bucket" / "Shared, per-process limiter"). (2) The rate_limit dependency is attached ONLY to POST /runs (app.py:273) and POST /sources/resolve (app.py:284); I verified all other routes (GET /dashboard, /runs, /runs/{id}, /news, /sources, /watchlist, /health; PUT /sources, /watchlist, /settings) carry only require_api_key/require_local, no rate_limit. (3) Collectors are unthrottled: net.py:74 safe_get contains only SSRF guards (no TokenBucket); rss.py, youtube.py, newsapi.py, scrape.py, feed_discovery.py, youtube_resolve.py all call safe_get directly. (4) The per-source promise is real and undelivered: spec §14 line 199 promises "Token-bucket rate limiters per external dependency (per news API, per LLM RPM, per host for scraping)", and plan4-sources.md:617 explicitly deferred wiring TokenBucket into collectors to "a later hardening pass" that never happened. (5) Cloud Run is the advertised prod target (README.md:11,18,45; spec §lines 80-83,262), currently "wired and unit-tested but not yet" deployed.

Severity correctly stays medium, not higher: the API is auth-gated when API_KEY is set (app.py:104-109 constant-time compare), default mode is single-user loopback desktop, and the multi-tenant Cloud Run path where cross-tenant DoS would bite is NOT yet deployed. So the global-bucket drain is an authenticated-caller / future-prod hardening gap, accurately medium. One minor wording nuance in the finding: the shared bucket lets any *authenticated* client (when a key is configured) drain POST runs/resolve for all callers — it is not anonymous cross-tenant DoS in the keyed deployment. The defaults (config.py:155-156) are burst=30, refill=1.0/s.

title: write_narrative() + top_n=12 ranker is dead on the runtime path — narration is actually unbounded
evidence: digest_editor.py:23-27 defines write_narrative(items, generate, top_n=12) which sorts by importance and truncates to the top 12. grep shows it is called ONLY from tests/unit/test_digest_editor.py — never from app code. The actual runtime narrator is _default_narrator -> adk_narrate (runner.py:100, agents.py:476) and DigestEditorAgent calls self.narrator(rendered) directly (agents.py:400). adk_narrate (digest_editor.py:41-48) applies NO top_n cap and feeds the full item list to the LLM.
impact: The 'top 12 items' truncation that the helper implements is never enforced at runtime, so the digest-editor prompt can be fed an unbounded item list (token-blowup / cost / latency risk on a large run). The helper and its test give a false sense that ranking/truncation exists.
recommendation: Either delete write_narrative + its two tests as dead code, OR have adk_narrate call write_narrative(items, generate, top_n=N) so the cap is actually applied (preferred — protects against unbounded narration input).
verifier_notes:
All evidence verified against the real code.

DEAD CODE: write_narrative() is defined at app/pipeline/digest_editor.py:23 with signature write_narrative(items, generate, top_n=12). Line 26 sorts by importance_score and slices [:top_n] — this is the ONLY place in app/ that sorts by importance and truncates. grep -rn "write_narrative" across all .py files returns exactly three hits: the definition (digest_editor.py:23) and two test calls (tests/unit/test_digest_editor.py:18 and :24). No app-code caller exists. Confirmed dead on the runtime path.

RUNTIME PATH (no cap): The actual narrator is adk_narrate. runner.py:99-100 _default_narrator returns lambda items: digest_editor.adk_narrate(items, settings). build_pipeline (agents.py:476) sets _narrator = narrator or _default_narrator(settings). DigestEditorAgent._run_async_impl (agents.py:399-400) calls self.narrator(rendered) directly via asyncio.to_thread. adk_narrate (digest_editor.py:41-48) json.dumps the FULL items list and feeds it to the LLM with no sort, no slice, no top_n — confirmed unbounded.

INPUT NOT OTHERWISE BOUNDED: rendered = select_rendered(items) (agents.py:395). select_rendered (runner.py:111-113) only filters by status (processed, else non-flagged); it imposes no count limit. So the digest-editor prompt can be fed an arbitrarily long item list.

Finding is fully accurate. Severity medium is fair: the dead-code / false-safety-signal aspect is unambiguous and the test gives a misleading impression that ranking+truncation is enforced. The cost/latency 'token-blowup' impact is real but conditional on large runs and partially mitigated by a per-LLM-call llm_timeout (referenced in runner.py:137), which bounds latency per call though not total input size or token cost. Keeping medium rather than high.

title: Multi-tenant org_id/user_id is vestigial scaffolding — stored but queried by nothing; user_id not even persisted
evidence: domain.py:134-135,175 define org_id/user_id on NewsItem and org_id on DigestRun (DEFAULT_ORG/DEFAULT_USER). sqlite_backend.py writes org_id as a column (lines 46,51,79,83,109,110) but grep shows ZERO WHERE/filter clauses using org_id in any list_news/list_runs/dashboard query. user_id does NOT appear in sqlite_backend.py at all (no column, never persisted). firestore_backend.py has ZERO references to org_id or user_id. frontend digestRunSchema (schemas.ts:61) declares org_id but no screen scopes by it (Zod also strips unknown user_id).
impact: Decorative multi-tenancy: the fields ride on every model and consume a DB column but provide zero isolation — any 'tenant' would see all data. This is dead weight that oversells the v1 capability and complicates the domain.
recommendation: For a single-user v1, DROP org_id/user_id from the domain models, the SQLite columns, and the frontend schema. Re-introduce them only when a real tenancy query path is built. If kept for true forward-compat, document them as inert and stop advertising multi-tenant in the spec.
verifier_notes: All cited claims verified against source. domain.py:134-135 define org_id/user_id on NewsItem (DEFAULT_ORG/DEFAULT_USER); domain.py:175 defines org_id on DigestRun (no user_id on DigestRun). sqlite_backend.py writes org_id as a column (lines 46,51,79,83,109,110) but grep confirms ZERO WHERE/filter clauses on org_id anywhere — list_news (132-154), list_runs (124-130), get_items_for_run (94-103), existing_ids (65-73) filter only by category/importance/status/run_id. user_id has 0 matches in sqlite_backend.py: it is NOT persisted as a column and i.user_id is never read in save_items; it survives only inside the opaque model_dump_json() data blob. firestore_backend.py has 0 references to org_id/user_id (no where() filters), though note both fields ARE persisted there via item.model_dump(mode='json') on line 48 — a minor nuance that doesn't change the no-filtering conclusion. schemas.ts:36-37 declare org_id+user_id on newsItemSchema; schemas.ts:61 declares org_id on digestRunSchema but omits user_id, and no screen scopes by either. The user_id='system' hits in runner.py/runtime.py/search.py/app_utils/typing.py are ADK session IDs, unrelated to the domain tenancy field, so they do not undercut the finding. Net: genuine vestigial/decorative multi-tenancy providing zero isolation — confirmed. Medium is the correct ceiling: it's latent dead scaffolding (oversells v1, complicates the domain) in an effectively single-tenant app, not an active exploitable isolation bypass, so not high.

title: YouTube summarizer bypasses the hardened JSON parser every other LLM stage uses
evidence: youtube.py:211 does `DigestNarrative.model_validate_json(raw)` directly on the raw model text. Every other LLM stage routes the same DigestNarrative/result through parse_model_json (e.g. digest_editor.py:48 `parse_model_json(text, DigestNarrative)`), which strips code fences and extracts balanced JSON before validation. This duplicates the parse-intent but with a fragile implementation.
impact: If Gemini wraps the YouTube summary in ```json fences or adds prose (which the rest of the codebase explicitly defends against), model_validate_json raises and the YouTube collector item fails — re-creating exactly the fragility parse.py was built to eliminate. Inconsistent contract across stages.
recommendation: Replace youtube.py:211 with `return parse_model_json(raw, DigestNarrative).narrative` to reuse the shared hardened path. Removes the duplicate/divergent parsing logic.
verifier_notes:
Finding is accurate on every cited point. app/services/youtube.py:211 (adk_summarize) does `DigestNarrative.model_validate_json(raw).narrative` directly on the raw text from run_agent_text — no fence-stripping, no balanced-JSON extraction. Every other LLM stage routes through the hardened parse_model_json: app/pipeline/digest_editor.py:48 (DigestNarrative), processing.py:66 & :114 (ProcessingResult), judge.py:70 (EnrichmentVerdicts), critic.py:71 (FaithfulnessVerdicts). parse.py's parse_model_json (lines 88-111) strips ```json/``` fences (_strip_code_fences) and extracts the first balanced JSON value (_extract_first_json_value) with a two-stage repair path before validating — exactly the robustness the YouTube path skips. The duplication-of-intent claim holds: youtube.py reimplements the parse step with the fragile primitive parse.py was built to replace.

Stronger than the auditor argued: the YouTube summarizer agent (youtube.py:200) and the digest_editor agent (digest_editor.py:35) are near-identical — both use output_schema=DigestNarrative, output via run_agent_text, and want narrative back. digest_editor STILL defends with parse_model_json despite output_schema, proving the codebase deliberately does not trust output_schema/controlled-generation to guarantee fence-free JSON. So the 'output_schema makes raw validate-safe' counter-argument is refuted by the codebase's own convention.

Impact is real but bounded. In collect() (youtube.py:259-282) summarize() is called per-video at line 268 with no try/except, so a single fenced/prose-wrapped Gemini response would raise out of adk_summarize and abort the whole channel's collection run (not just one item). That's contained to one collector but worse than a single-item skip. output_schema lowers (does not eliminate) the trigger probability, so severity sits at the low end of medium — I'd keep medium given the unguarded per-channel blast radius and the clean, low-risk fix (swap line 211 to parse_model_json(raw, DigestNarrative).narrative, already imported elsewhere).

title: Cloud Run container serves /api/* but ships NO frontend — the 'GCP webapp' is a backend with no UI
evidence: Dockerfile COPYs only ./app and ./config (lines 23,27) — never ./frontend, never runs npm/next build, has no node toolchain. The deploy entrypoint fast_api_app.py:77 calls register_product_routes(app, _settings) (mounts /api/*) but NEVER calls mount_console() — only app/api/app.py:367 (create_app, the desktop path) does. frontend/out is gitignored (.gitignore:29, frontend/.gitignore:18), so even the built export isn't in the image context. The console is a static export (next.config.ts:7 output:'export') that the desktop launcher builds at runtime (run.sh:132 NEXT_PUBLIC_API_BASE='' npm run build).
impact: Anyone who runs `agents-cli deploy` / docker-builds and hits the Cloud Run service gets the ADK agent web UI + bare JSON /api/* endpoints, but the actual product console (Dashboard/News/Digests/Sources/Watchlist/Settings) is absent. The README claims 'a deployed Next.js console reaches a real backend' (fast_api_app.py:73-74 comment) but the deployed container has no console to serve. This is a primary contributor to the 'gcp webapp feels disconnected' complaint.
recommendation: Pick one: (a) add a Node build stage to the Dockerfile that runs `cd frontend && NEXT_PUBLIC_API_BASE='' npm ci && npm run build`, COPY frontend/out into the image, and call mount_console() in fast_api_app.py — OR (b) deploy the frontend separately to Vercel/Cloud-Run-static and set ALLOW_ORIGINS for it, OR (c) drop the cloud-console claim entirely and document the container as API-only. Do not leave the comment in fast_api_app.py:73-74 promising a behavior the Dockerfile makes impossible.
verifier_notes: Every technical claim verifies against the code. Dockerfile (lines 21/23/27) COPYs only pyproject/README/uv.lock, ./app, ./config — never ./frontend, no npm/next build, no node toolchain; CMD = uvicorn app.fast_api_app:app. The deploy entrypoint app/fast_api_app.py builds the app via get_fast_api_app(web=True) and at line 77 calls register_product_routes (mounts /api/*), but NEVER mounts a static console. git grep confirms mount_console( is called in EXACTLY one place repo-wide: app/api/app.py:367, inside create_app() (the desktop path), gated on Path(settings.console_dir).is_dir(). console_dir defaults to REPO_ROOT/frontend/out (app/core/config.py:153). next.config.ts:7 output:'export' confirmed. frontend/out is gitignored (root .gitignore:29 'out/', frontend/.gitignore:18 '/out/') and `git ls-files frontend/out` returns nothing (it exists only as a local build artifact on disk). The launcher builds it at runtime — finding cited run.sh:132 but the actual path is scripts/run.sh:132 (content exact: `( cd frontend && npm ci && NEXT_PUBLIC_API_BASE=\"\" npm run build )`); the bare `run.sh` at repo root does not exist (cosmetic path-prefix error only). The fast_api_app.py:73-74 comment verbatim claims the deployed /api/* exists 'so a deployed Next.js console ... reaches a real backend,' yet that container ships no console. There is no .dockerignore, but it's moot — frontend/ is never COPYed anyway. So the deployed Cloud Run image truly serves the ADK web UI + bare JSON /api/* with NO product console (Dashboard/News/Digests/Sources/Watchlist/Settings). Severity downgraded high->medium because this is largely by-design, not a malfunction: fast_api_app.py's own docstring (lines 15-29) declares this surface the ADK *agent* deployment target (Agent Engine/Cloud Run with ADK web UI), and the README frames the product console as the local desktop/single-port experience (README:82-92). Console-on-Cloud-Run was never a documented deliverable of the Docker/agents-cli deploy path. It's a genuine deployment-completeness gap and a plausible contributor to 'gcp webapp feels disconnected,' but not a broken/regressed feature.

title: Deployed container ignores Settings for sessions — hardcoded in-memory, breaking the 'swap by config only' promise at the deploy surface
evidence: fast_api_app.py:57-58 hardcodes `session_service_uri = None` (in-memory ADK sessions) and passes it to get_fast_api_app — it never reads Settings.session_backend / session_db_url. The durable path exists ONLY in the local code: runner.py:74-78 make_session_service() honors settings.session_backend ('database' default, config.py:106) and resolves a SQLite/aiosqlite URL. So the well-tested DatabaseSessionService durability (proved in tests/integration/test_run_digest_database_session.py) is plumbed into run_digest/create_app but NOT into the deployed ADK surface. Same gap for storage: config default storage_backend='sqlite', sqlite_path=REPO_ROOT/data/catchup.db (config.py:81-82) — but the deploy container's agent (loaded via get_fast_api_app(agents_dir=AGENT_DIR)) writes SQLite to the container's ephemeral filesystem.
impact: The central design promise ('SQLite->Firestore, AI Studio->Vertex, APScheduler->Cloud Scheduler by config alone, no rewrite') is real for the local process and false for the only deployable artifact. A deployed run would use in-memory ADK sessions and write product SQLite to ephemeral disk that vanishes on container restart/redeploy — exactly the 'agents vs sqlite all scattered' incoherence. The docs' 'config-only swap' becomes a lie at the deployment boundary.
recommendation: Make fast_api_app.py read Settings: pass make_session_service-derived session_service_uri (or the DatabaseSessionService) instead of hardcoding None, and ensure storage_backend is respected (Firestore for cloud, or a mounted volume for SQLite). Better: collapse to ONE ASGI app so there is a single place where storage/session config is applied. If the cloud path is deferred, document fast_api_app.py as ephemeral-demo-only.
verifier_notes:
The raw facts are accurate but the impact is materially overstated.

CONFIRMED facts:
- app/fast_api_app.py:57-58 does hardcode `session_service_uri = None` and never reads Settings.session_backend/session_db_url.
- app/runner.py:74-78 make_session_service() honors settings.session_backend (config.py:106 default "database") and resolves a sqlite+aiosqlite URL via _resolve_session_db_url.
- config.py:81-82 storage defaults to sqlite at REPO_ROOT/data/catchup.db; Dockerfile copies ./app and ./config but NOT ./data, writing to ephemeral disk.
- tests/integration/test_run_digest_database_session.py exists.

WHY OVERSTATED:
1. The claim "A deployed run would use in-memory ADK sessions" conflates two distinct surfaces. The deployed container ALSO mounts the product /api/* routes via register_product_routes(app, _settings) (fast_api_app.py:77). The product's real digest runs go through POST /api/runs (app/api/app.py:273-278) -> try_start_run(..., run_digest_fn=run_digest) -> run_digest (runner.py:173) -> make_session_service(settings), which DOES honor session_backend="database" -> DatabaseSessionService. So the well-tested durability IS reachable on the deployed artifact. session_service_uri=None only governs ADK's own web-UI / /run interactive agent sessions (a developer playground surface), not the pipeline's session state. The pipeline never reads session_service_uri.

2. Storage IS config-honored on the deployed container: register_product_routes uses build_storage(settings) per request (app.py:157-158), which respects storage_backend (sqlite/firestore). The documented production path is storage_backend="firestore" (docs/ADK-GUIDE.md:158). The SQLite-to-ephemeral-disk problem only manifests under default config — a footgun, not a hardcoded lie.

3. The "config-only swap is a lie at deployment" framing contradicts documented scope: docs/ADK-GUIDE.md:165 explicitly states the milestone target is local/self-hosted and the App (Agent Engine / Cloud Run) path is FUTURE. The README/spec swap promise is about ports/interfaces (storage, provider, scheduler) — all honored by the deployed container.

GENUINE RESIDUAL DEFECT (justifies medium, not refute): the ADK agent surface's session_service_uri is hardcoded None and ignores Settings.session_backend/session_db_url, so the ADK web/interactive surface always uses in-memory sessions regardless of config. That is a real, narrow config-drift at one surface — worth a one-line fix to derive session_service_uri from make_session_service / _resolve_session_db_url when session_backend=="database" — but it does NOT break the product pipeline's durability and is not a high-severity architecture failure given the documented local-first deploy scope.

title: agent.py performs SQLite schema-init side effects at import time — fires inside the deployed ADK container
evidence: agent.py:24-25 runs at module import: `_settings = Settings()` then `build_pipeline(_settings, build_storage(_settings), run_id=...)`. build_storage (runner.py:52-62) constructs SqliteBackend(settings.sqlite_path) and calls store.init_schema() — creating/opening data/catchup.db. The deploy container loads this agent because fast_api_app.py:62-63 calls get_fast_api_app(agents_dir=AGENT_DIR), which imports the `app` package's agent. So merely importing the deployed app writes a SQLite file to the container's ephemeral filesystem and builds a throwaway pipeline whose run_id is immediately discarded (run_digest generates its own).
impact: Import-time DB/filesystem writes in a Cloud Run container are fragile (read-only or ephemeral FS, cold-start cost, surprising side effects) and reinforce the storage incoherence. The work is wasted (the pipeline/run_id built here is never used by the product run path). It also makes the module non-importable in any read-only environment.
recommendation: Make agent.py side-effect-free at import: build root_agent lazily or with an in-memory/no-init storage stub, and do not call build_storage()/init_schema() at module scope. Generating the throwaway run_id at import (agent.py:16,25) is already dead per build_pipeline's own contract — drop it. Storage init belongs to the run path (runner.run_digest), not the agent module.
verifier_notes:
Every factual claim verified against the real code and empirically reproduced.

1. agent.py:24-25 — at MODULE SCOPE (not in a function): `_settings = Settings()` then `root_agent = build_pipeline(_settings, build_storage(_settings), run_id=uuid.uuid4().hex[:12])`. Confirmed.

2. build_storage (runner.py:52-62): default backend is sqlite, constructs `SqliteBackend(settings.sqlite_path)` (line 55) then calls `store.init_schema()` (line 61). Confirmed.

3. SqliteBackend side effects: __init__ (sqlite_backend.py:19-21) does `Path(path).parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)` — creates the data/ dir at construction. init_schema (42-63) opens `sqlite3.connect(self.path)` (creating the .db file), sets PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL (creating -wal/-shm), and runs CREATE TABLE/CREATE INDEX DDL. So importing the module writes to the filesystem. Confirmed.

4. Defaults make this fire by default: config.py storage_backend="sqlite" (L81), sqlite_path = REPO_ROOT/data/catchup.db (L82). No STORAGE_BACKEND override exists in any .tf/.yaml/Dockerfile/env file (grep found none), so the deployed container really runs the sqlite path.

5. Deploy load chain confirmed and stronger than the finding states: app/__init__.py:15 is `from .agent import app`, so importing the `app` package alone executes agent.py's module-level code. fast_api_app.py:62-63 calls get_fast_api_app(agents_dir=AGENT_DIR) which imports the app package agent; fast_api_app.py also directly imports app.api.app and app.core.config, all of which trigger app/__init__ -> agent import. Empirically: `import app` (with no other call) created the DB file at the configured path (probe printed "db exists at import-time path: True").

6. run_id is genuinely discarded: agent.py builds a pipeline with a fresh uuid at import; the product run path run_digest (runner.py:146-198) generates its OWN run_id (L164) and builds its OWN pipeline (L165). Import-time pipeline/run_id are unused. Confirmed.

7. "Non-importable in read-only env" impact confirmed empirically: pointing SQLITE_PATH at an unwritable root makes `import app` crash with OSError [Errno 30] Read-only file system (raised at the mkdir in SqliteBackend.__init__).

Severity calibration: medium is correct. It is a real architecture/correctness defect (import-time DB/FS writes, wasted throwaway pipeline, fragility, hard crash on read-only FS), but Cloud Run's default filesystem is a writable in-memory tmpfs, so the default-config deployment does not crash — it wastes a little RAM/cold-start and leaves an unused DB. It only becomes a hard failure when the FS is configured read-only or the path is unwritable. Not overstated; not under-stated.

title: Firestore backend has ZERO real-store coverage; the emulator gate is a permanently-skipped stub that raises AssertionError
evidence: test_firestore_emulator.py:23-29 — `pytestmark = pytest.mark.skip(...)` and the single test body is `raise AssertionError("implement against the emulator before deploy")`. It is the ONLY skip that fires at runtime. test_firestore_backend.py:11 constructs `FirestoreBackend(FakeFirestoreClient())` — the fake's own docstring (fake_firestore.py:1-4) says 'no real query semantics (no composite-index or inequality-filter constraints)'. The shared StorageContract (storage_contract.py) always seeds items WITH a status, so the documented SQLite-NULL vs Firestore `is_flagged == False`-doesn't-match-missing-field data-loss divergence (recorded in test_firestore_emulator.py:14-16) is never exercised by any test.
impact: The headline 'swap SQLite->Firestore by config only' claim is UNTESTED against any real store. The first real Firestore query in prod could fail on a missing composite index or silently drop docs whose is_flagged field is absent — exactly the data loss the stub warns about. The contract-green status is misleading: it proves adapter logic, not Firestore compatibility.
recommendation: Either (a) implement test_firestore_emulator.py against `gcloud beta emulators firestore` and wire it into a pre-deploy CI job (the checklist is already written in the file), and add a contract case for a missing-is_flagged doc; or (b) until then, mark FirestoreBackend 'experimental/unsupported' in the README so the test status matches reality. Do not let contract-green imply prod-ready.
verifier_notes:
Core technical claims VERIFIED, but the "ZERO coverage" headline and "ONLY skip" sub-claim are overstated.

CONFIRMED:
- test_firestore_emulator.py:23-29: unconditional pytestmark = pytest.mark.skip(...), body raises AssertionError("implement against the emulator before deploy"). The only real-store/emulator test is permanently skipped.
- fake_firestore.py:1-4 docstring confirms "no real query semantics (no composite-index or inequality-filter constraints)". The fake (FakeQuery.where) only supports '=='; raises NotImplementedError otherwise. Cannot surface missing-index failures.
- Missing-field divergence is genuinely UNtested: NewsItem.status defaults to "raw" (domain.py:153) and FirestoreBackend._item_doc (firestore_backend.py:47-50) ALWAYS writes is_flagged. The fake stores only what the adapter writes, so no doc ever lacks is_flagged. SQLite handles legacy rows via "(status IS NULL OR status != 'flagged')" (sqlite_backend.py:99,144) while Firestore uses is_flagged == False (firestore_backend.py:67,94) — equality does NOT match a missing field. This SQLite-NULL vs Firestore-missing-field data-loss divergence, documented at test_firestore_emulator.py:14-16, is exercised by no test. Confirmed real.
- Positional where(field,op,value) is deprecated in real Firestore (adapter docstring + emulator checklist line 9-10) and untested against the real API.

OVERSTATED / REFUTED parts:
- "ZERO real-store coverage" / "contract-green is misleading" — adapter logic is actually well covered: 11 tests in test_firestore_backend.py PASS (ran them) via the SHARED StorageContract that SQLite also subclasses (filters, ordering, pagination, flagged-exclusion, is_flagged derivation, batch writes). So it's zero REAL-Firestore coverage, not zero coverage. The contract correctly proves adapter logic; it never claims to prove Firestore compatibility.
- "It is the ONLY skip that fires at runtime" — FALSE. Two other skips exist: test_build_storage.py:35 and test_pipeline_persistent_session.py:95 (conditional pytest.skip in test bodies). Minor inaccuracy.

SEVERITY: lowered high->medium. The data-integrity risk is real but it is a KNOWN, DOCUMENTED, GATED limitation: the skipped emulator test serves as an explicit pre-deploy gate with a 5-step checklist (un-skip, swap to FieldFilter, create composite indexes, backfill is_flagged=False, keep UTC ISO timestamps). No test falsely asserts real-store compatibility, and nothing is in production yet. It is a tracked pre-deploy hole, not a silent active bug — medium, not high.

title: Frontend mocks fetch wholesale — no test verifies Zod schemas match real backend response shapes; schemas.ts/categories.ts/hooks.ts untested
evidence: api.test.ts:8-13 etc. stub global fetch with hand-written payloads; api.test.ts:78-88 proves Zod REJECTS a malformed shape — but the 'correct' shapes in the tests are authored by the test, not derived from the backend. So the schemas are validated against themselves. Verified that lib/schemas.ts, lib/categories.ts, lib/hooks.ts have NO matching *.test.ts (contradicting the mapping's claim that every helper has a test). The backend↔frontend shape agreement (e.g. category_counts enum keys, settings fields, the 5th 'youtube' source type, org_id/user_id) is maintained by hand with no automated guard.
impact: If the FastAPI response shape changes (renamed field, new required key, changed enum value), the frontend Zod schema will start throwing ApiError(status:0) at runtime for real users, but the entire frontend test suite stays green because it never sees a real backend response. This is a classic false-confidence vector across the API seam.
recommendation: Add a contract test that runs the real FastAPI TestClient (or a committed sample of its JSON responses generated by the backend tests) and parses it through the frontend Zod schemas — even a small fixture file shared between backend and frontend would catch drift. At minimum add unit tests for schemas.ts (round-trip a representative payload) and categories.ts.
verifier_notes:
Verified all four claims against the real code.

1) Untested helpers: CONFIRMED. frontend/lib/ contains tests only for api, format, grouping, sources, tags, use-language. schemas.ts, categories.ts, and hooks.ts have NO matching *.test.ts/.tsx. The mapping's "every helper has a test" claim is contradicted.

2) Schemas validated against self-authored payloads: CONFIRMED. api.test.ts stubs global.fetch with hand-written payloads (e.g. line 7 the "good" DashboardOut, lines 80/83 the "bad" one). The malformed-shape rejection test (lines 78-88) asserts ApiError.status===0, but both the valid and invalid shapes are authored by the test, never derived from FastAPI. The Zod schemas are validated against themselves.

3) No automated backend<->frontend contract guard: CONFIRMED. frontend/package.json scripts are only dev/build/start/lint/test(vitest) — no openapi/orval/swagger/codegen. schemas.ts's own header comment says it "mirrors lib/types.ts" (a manual mirror). Backend app/api/schemas.py DashboardOut.category_counts is dict[str,int]; frontend dashboardOutSchema uses z.record(z.string(), z.number()). The 5th source type "youtube" (app/core/domain.py:18 SourceType.YOUTUBE) and org_id/user_id (newsItemSchema) are kept in sync by hand. Agreement is maintained manually with no test.

4) Runtime failure path: CONFIRMED. api.ts:72-80 — on schema.safeParse failure it throws new ApiError(0, "Received an unexpected response from the server.", ...). Drift in a required/typed field => status:0 ApiError for real users, while the suite stays green.

Minor nuance (does not change severity): the schemas are intentionally loose in the highest-drift spots — category_counts is z.record(z.string(), z.number()) and source_errors is z.record(z.string(), z.unknown()), so a renamed/added category KEY would pass validation silently (different failure mode than the "throws at runtime" impact for that field; for renamed/removed required NewsItem/DigestRun fields the throw-at-runtime impact holds exactly). The schemas do provide genuine boundary defense, so they are not worthless — but the contract-drift coverage gap and the three untested helpers are real. Medium severity stands; not overstated enough to downgrade.

title: The Gemini -> run_agent_text -> parse_model_json -> schema chain is tested only in disconnected pieces; the eval baseline is fully synthetic and never live-validated
evidence: Every integration test injects the processor/critic/narrator callable (e.g. test_run_digest.py:42-44, test_pipeline_tree.py:77-84), so adk_enrich/adk_critique — which compose the prompt, call run_agent_text, and run parse_model_json — are bypassed. test_llm_runtime/test_adk_runtime fake `_run_text_async`, and test_llm_parse feeds parse_model_json hand-written JSON. No test connects a realistic model text output through run_agent_text into parse_model_json into the ItemEnrichment/ProcessingResult schema. baseline.json is n=35 with all-passing synthetic verdicts; test_eval_harness uses fake judges; the live judge loop has by the docs' own admission never run (quota-deferred).
impact: A silent disagreement between a prompt template, an agent output_schema, and parse_model_json (e.g. a renamed field, a new enum value, a category the prompt emits that the schema rejects) would not be caught by any offline test — only by a live Gemini run, which has never been executed in the eval loop. The 'structured output is load-bearing' question (output_schema AND parse_model_json both run) is unresolved by tests.
recommendation: Add one offline 'realistic model output' test that feeds a hand-crafted but representative Gemini-style JSON (with code fences, an extra field, etc.) through the actual adk_enrich parse path into ItemEnrichment, asserting the full chain. Separately, run the live eval loop once when quota allows and replace the synthetic baseline.json with a real one (or label it clearly as synthetic).
verifier_notes:
Attempted to refute; the finding holds on every point against the real code.

CHAIN IS TESTED ONLY IN DISCONNECTED PIECES — confirmed:
- The real chain lives in app/pipeline/processing.py:62-66 adk_enrich = build_processing_agent (output_schema=ProcessingResult, line 56) -> run_agent_text (line 65) -> parse_model_json(text, ProcessingResult) (line 66). adk_reprocess (105-114), critic adk_critique, judge adk_judge (judge.py:60-69), narrator adk_narrate are structured identically.
- grep across tests/ shows NO test ever references adk_enrich / adk_reprocess / adk_critique / adk_narrate / adk_judge. Zero. The real composed functions are never called offline.
- Integration tests inject already-built schema objects, bypassing the chain: test_run_digest.py passes processor=lambda items: ProcessingResult(items=[]), narrator=lambda items: "", critic=lambda items: []. test_pipeline_tree.py injects a fake_processor returning hand-built ProcessingResult(ItemEnrichment(...)). agents.py (build_pipeline, 453-543) and runner.run_digest expose processor/narrator/critic/reprocessor as injectable params defaulting to _default_* (runner.py:95-108) — so the default LLM path is replaceable and is replaced in every test.
- test_llm_runtime.py and test_pipeline_live_bridge.py monkeypatch runtime._run_text_async to return canned strings ("OK"/"canned model output") — they test the retry/timeout/loop-bridge logic of run_agent_text, never a realistic model JSON through to a schema.
- test_llm_parse.py feeds hand-written JSON literals to parse_model_json against throwaway _Item/_Result/_Verdicts models — never the production ItemEnrichment/ProcessingResult schema, and never text that originated from the prompt/agent.
- test_judge.py:84 test_enrichment_verdicts_round_trip only does ev.model_dump_json() -> model_validate_json() on a hand-built object; no run_agent_text, no parse_model_json. test_judge.py:40 only asserts the agent's output_schema/name.
- No test connects realistic model text -> run_agent_text -> parse_model_json -> ItemEnrichment/ProcessingResult. The 'output_schema AND parse_model_json both load-bearing' question is unresolved offline. processing.py:63 even comments adk_enrich is "Validated by the live smoke (needs GOOGLE_API_KEY)".

BASELINE IS SYNTHETIC, NEVER LIVE-VALIDATED — confirmed:
- tests/eval/baseline.json is n=35, passed=true, failures=[] with round pass-rates (faithfulness 1.0 etc).
- The eval unit tests (tests/unit/test_eval_harness.py) run via fake_enrich + fake_judge/_perfect/_zero/_gold; verdicts carry reason="synthetic" (line 20). tests/unit/test_eval_score.py only tests aggregate/compare math on hand-built _report(...) EvalReports.
- Docs confirm the live loop has never run: docs/eval/README.md:15 "Live (requires GOOGLE_API_KEY — deferred until quota is available)"; :87 run --live "when quota is available" to get real scores; docs/ADK-GUIDE.md:151 "Tests inject fakes (synthetic Pydantic results / fixtures)... real adk_* functions are the live path, validated by a live smoke when a key is present"; plan8 "Deferred to quota: a live end-to-end tree run." scripts/eval_enrichment.py:213 hard-requires GOOGLE_API_KEY for live mode and prints "Offline mode: no live model calls" otherwise.

SEVERITY: medium is correct, not higher. Mitigating: output_schema=ProcessingResult is set server-side on every agent (constrains the model), the adk_* wrappers are intentionally ~3 lines each, the parse/schema/retry components are individually well-tested, and a real live smoke path (scripts/eval_enrichment.py --live, --calibrate, --check-regression) exists — it is simply unexecuted due to Gemini quota. So this is a genuine test-coverage/eval-validation incompleteness (a prompt/schema/parser disagreement would escape all offline tests, and the baseline numbers are placeholders) rather than a live defect. Not low (the integration seam truly has zero offline coverage and the baseline is non-empirical), not high (no evidence of an actual mismatch; components are correct in isolation).

Relevant files: app/pipeline/processing.py, /app/pipeline/judge.py, /app/runner.py, /app/pipeline/agents.py, /tests/unit/test_llm_runtime.py, /tests/unit/test_llm_parse.py, /tests/unit/test_judge.py, /tests/unit/test_eval_harness.py, /tests/unit/test_eval_score.py, /tests/integration/test_pipeline_live_bridge.py, /tests/integration/test_run_digest.py, /tests/integration/test_pipeline_tree.py, /tests/eval/baseline.json, /scripts/eval_enrichment.py, /docs/eval/README.md, /docs/ADK-GUIDE.md

title: app/.env overrides model (gemini-2.5-flash-lite vs documented gemini-flash-latest)
evidence: config.py line 87 default gemini-flash-latest; README 167 and ADK-GUIDE 5 (do not change) agree. app/.env line 8 LLM_MODEL gemini-2.5-flash-lite wins; live Settings resolves to it. app/.env is gitignored, not committed.
impact: Local runs use a different model than every doc and the CLAUDE.md never-change-model rule - silent reproducibility gap.
recommendation: Align the source default, docs, and app/.env on one model.
verifier_notes: Every factual claim verified against the real code. app/core/config.py:87 default is exactly `llm_model: str = "gemini-flash-latest"`. docs/ADK-GUIDE.md:5 states `gemini-flash-latest (do not change)`; README.md:167 documents `LLM_MODEL | gemini-flash-latest` and line 274 reinforces keeping the alias unchanged. app/.env:8 contains `LLM_MODEL=gemini-2.5-flash-lite`. git confirms app/.env is gitignored (.gitignore:2 `.env` matches `app/.env`) and not tracked. Live execution of `Settings().llm_model` returns `gemini-2.5-flash-lite`, proving the override resolves on this machine and diverges from every doc plus the CLAUDE.md/README never-change-model rule. One minor mechanism imprecision in the EVIDENCE: it attributes the win to env_file precedence, but the tuple is `("app/.env", ".env")` and pydantic-settings gives the LATER file priority — so a root .env would actually win. There is no root .env (confirmed absent), so app/.env is the only env file and resolves to flash-lite anyway; the conclusion is correct, only the why is slightly off. Severity stays medium (not higher): the override is intentional, documented inline in app/.env:5-7 with quota rationale, gitignored, and machine-local — zero impact on committed state, CI, or other contributors. The reproducibility-gap impact is real but bounded to one developer's box, so medium is appropriate (arguably low-medium border given the deliberate, self-documented nature).

title: SQLite connections never closed; build_storage re-runs DDL per request
evidence: Queries use a with-block over self._conn (sqlite_backend.py:69,76,101,118,125,148). A sqlite3 context manager does NOT close the connection; no conn.close in the file. API builds a backend per request (app.py:157-158) which calls init_schema (runner.py:61).
impact: Leaks connections/fds and re-runs DDL on every request.
recommendation: Close via try/finally; build the backend once in lifespan.
verifier_notes:
The finding bundles two claims; the severity-driving one (fd/connection leak) is refuted, the other (DDL per request) is real but low.

CLAIM 1 — "connections never closed → leaks fds" — REFUTED. The auditor mischaracterizes the code. `_conn` is NOT a stored attribute; it is a FACTORY METHOD (sqlite_backend.py:23-31, `def _conn(self) -> sqlite3.Connection: ... return conn`) that opens a BRAND-NEW connection on each call. Every cited line (69, 76, 101, 118, 125, 148 — plus 43, 106) uses `with self._conn() as conn:` (note the PARENTHESES = method call), not `with self._conn`. Each connection is a short-lived local scoped to its `with` block; on method return it is dereferenced and reclaimed by CPython refcounting (sqlite3 closes the underlying handle in __del__). It is never held on `self`, so connections do NOT accumulate — there is no unbounded fd/connection leak. The only true sub-point is that there is no explicit `conn.close()` (the sqlite3 context manager only commits/rolls back, it does not close) — that is a minor cleanliness nit, not a leak. So the IMPACT "Leaks connections/fds" does not hold.

CLAIM 2 — "build_storage re-runs DDL per request" — CONFIRMED but LOW. app.py:157-158 `def storage(): return build_storage(settings)` is invoked per request, and build_storage (runner.py:52-62) calls `store.init_schema()` every time (runner.py:61). init_schema (sqlite_backend.py:42-63) runs CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS / CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS / PRAGMA table_info migration checks. All are idempotent and cheap once the schema exists; this is a minor per-request inefficiency, not a correctness bug or resource-exhaustion issue.

Net: the "high" rating rests on the leak claim, which is false; the surviving DDL-per-request issue is a low-severity perf nit.

title: Retry loop retries non-transient failures
evidence: run_agent_text runtime.py:83-109 catches bare except Exception (line 100) and retries all errors including 401, 404, ValidationError.
impact: Fatal config errors burn the retry budget on every LLM call.
recommendation: Retry only timeouts/5xx/429; re-raise auth/404/ValidationError.
verifier_notes:
Technical claim is accurate. In app/llm/runtime.py the function run_agent_text spans lines 83-109; line 100 is a bare `except Exception as exc` whose inline comment claims it only catches "transient runner/timeout failures," but it actually catches every exception including non-transient ones (401 auth, 404 model-not-found, ValidationError). Retry budget = 1 + max(0, llm_max_retries); config.py default llm_max_retries=2 so attempts=3, with llm_backoff_base=0.5 and llm_timeout=60. So a fatal config error is retried 3x with ~1.5s total backoff sleep before re-raising, at every LLM call site (judge.py:69, processing.py:65/113, digest_editor.py:47, critic.py:70, youtube.py:210).

Why the medium severity is overstated to low: (1) the last exception is always re-raised (lines 102-103 break + line 109 `raise last_exc`), so fatal errors are NOT swallowed — only delayed. (2) The waste is bounded and small: at most 2 extra LLM round-trips plus ~1.5s sleep per call; no infinite loop, no data corruption, no security impact. For an immediate 401/404 rejection the extra calls are fast and cheap; the main cost is the deliberate time.sleep. This is a legitimate efficiency/code-clarity bug (over-broad catch contradicting its own comment) but minor in real impact — appropriate severity is low, not medium. Confirmed-but-overstated => partial.

title: Flagged-exclusion missing-field semantics diverge between backends (SQLite NULL-safe, Firestore drops field-missing docs)
evidence: SQLite's default-exclude clause is NULL-tolerant: sqlite_backend.py:100 and :145 use "(status IS NULL OR status != 'flagged')", so rows whose status field is absent/NULL are RETURNED. Firestore's equivalent is firestore_backend.py:67,94 "where('is_flagged', '==', False)" over a derived boolean (line 49: data['is_flagged'] = item.status == 'flagged'). Real Firestore '== False' does NOT match documents that lack the is_flagged field entirely — so any doc written before the is_flagged convention (or by a different writer) would be SILENTLY EXCLUDED from default queries, whereas SQLite would include it. test_firestore_emulator.py:14-16 itself flags this as pre-deploy checklist step 4 ('Backfill is_flagged=False ... does NOT match a missing field — unlike SQLite NULL-status handling'). The fake never exercises this because it stores is_flagged on every write.
impact: Same StorageBackend port, two different read semantics for missing data: a default 'show me the news' query can return different result SETS depending on backend. On Firestore this manifests as silent data loss (legitimate non-flagged items vanish from the dashboard/news list) for any document missing the derived field — exactly the kind of subtle dual-storage inconsistency that erodes trust in the abstraction.
recommendation: Make the semantics identical and explicit. Either store is_flagged on 100% of writes AND run a one-time backfill (checklist step 4) before any query relies on it, or — better — model status uniformly: store the raw status string in Firestore too and filter status NOT IN ['flagged'] / use a status-in-allowlist query so both backends key off the same field with the same missing-value behavior. Add a StorageContract case asserting that a doc with NO status field is treated as not-flagged on BOTH backends (it would currently pass on the fake and fail on real Firestore).
verifier_notes:
The technical core of the finding is accurate and verifiable. SQLite (sqlite_backend.py:100, :145) uses `(status IS NULL OR status != 'flagged')`, which is NULL-tolerant — rows with absent/NULL status are RETURNED. Firestore (firestore_backend.py:67, :94) uses `where("is_flagged", "==", False)` over a derived boolean set at line 49 (`data["is_flagged"] = item.status == "flagged"`). Real Firestore equality filters do NOT match documents lacking the field, so a missing-field doc would be excluded there but included in SQLite — a genuine divergence in read semantics for the same StorageBackend port. The repo's OWN pre-deploy checklist documents this verbatim (test_firestore_emulator.py:14-16: "Backfill is_flagged=False ... does NOT match a missing field — unlike SQLite's NULL-status handling").

However, the "silent data loss" impact is overstated for the current state, justifying a downgrade from medium to low:
1. There is NO second writer. The only news_items writer is save_items, which always routes through _item_doc (line 49) and always sets is_flagged. No code path in this repo can produce a missing-field doc on fresh collections. The "written before the convention / by a different writer" scenario is purely hypothetical/migration-time.
2. The Firestore backend is optional and NOT deployed: test_firestore_emulator.py is `pytest.mark.skip`, the adapter still uses deprecated positional `where(field, op, value)` (line 8 / checklist step 2 says swap before deploy), and the file is explicitly "pre-deploy validation" that hasn't run. So no production query is affected today.
3. The team has already identified the exact issue AND its remediation (backfill is_flagged=False) in their checklist — it's a tracked latent migration footgun, not an active dual-storage bug.

Minor correction to the finding's reasoning: it claims "the fake never exercises this because it stores is_flagged on every write." The first half is right (no test inserts a missing-field doc, so it's untested), but fake_firestore.py:76 filters with `v.get(field) == value`, and `None == False` is False — so the fake WOULD actually replicate the real missing-field exclusion if such a doc were ever inserted; it just never is. Net: divergence real and well-documented, but it's a latent pre-deploy/migration risk on an un-deployed optional backend with no code path that triggers it — low severity.

title: build_storage() called per HTTP request — re-inits SQLite schema / rebuilds Firestore client on every API call
evidence: app/api/app.py:157-158 defines storage() as a per-call factory: 'def storage(): return build_storage(settings)'; every read route calls it fresh — dashboard() app.py:218, list_runs() :237, get_run() :241, list_news() :251. build_storage (runner.py:52-62) unconditionally calls store.init_schema() at line 61 on every construction. For SQLite that re-runs CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS + PRAGMA table_info introspection + CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS on every request; for Firestore _firestore_client() (runner.py:40-49) reconstructs a brand-new firestore.Client (a fresh gRPC channel) per request — and pre-deploy checklist step 5 (test_firestore_emulator.py:18-19) explicitly warns to cache one client per process under real API traffic.
impact: Wasteful but mostly benign on SQLite (cheap DDL no-ops, though every request opens a new connection and re-runs PRAGMA). On Firestore it is a real performance/cost problem: a new gRPC client + channel handshake per request under production traffic, no connection pooling, higher latency and resource churn. It also means there is no single long-lived storage instance — contradicting the repository-singleton implied by the hexagonal design.
recommendation: Build storage once at app startup (FastAPI lifespan or a module-level singleton keyed by settings) and inject it via Depends, calling init_schema() exactly once. This makes the SQLite path cleaner and is a hard prerequisite before Firestore can be considered production-ready (client caching).
verifier_notes:
Every cited line checks out verbatim. app/api/app.py:157-158 defines `def storage(): return build_storage(settings)` as a per-call factory, and read routes call it fresh: dashboard() :218, list_runs() :237, get_run() :241, list_news() :251. runner.py build_storage (52-62) unconditionally calls store.init_schema() at line 61 on every construction. For SQLite, init_schema() (sqlite_backend.py:42-63) re-runs CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS x2, PRAGMA table_info introspection via _ensure_columns, and 6x CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS — each method opens its own fresh sqlite3 connection with PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL, so a single request opens several connections. For Firestore, _firestore_client() (runner.py:40-49) builds a brand-new firestore.Client per call (its init_schema is a no-op at firestore_backend.py:37-38, so the cost is the client/gRPC channel construction, which the finding correctly attributes). test_firestore_emulator.py:18-19 confirms the warning verbatim: "consider caching one client per process under real API traffic (build_storage currently builds one per call)." The no-singleton / architecture-drift observation is also accurate.

Why I downgrade medium -> low: this is the local desktop app branch (feat/local-desktop-app) whose default storage_backend is "sqlite", where the DDL is cheap idempotent no-ops and SQLite connections are trivially cheap to open — the impact on the actual shipping path is negligible. The genuinely costly scenario (new gRPC client per request, no pooling) only occurs under the Firestore backend, which is gated behind an optional [firestore] extra AND is explicitly unvalidated/pre-deploy: the emulator test is hard-skipped and unimplemented (raises AssertionError), and the adapter still uses deprecated positional where() that the checklist says must be swapped before any real deploy. So the high-impact case is real but latent on an unshipped path, and the default path is benign. Real bug, correctly described, but "medium" over-weights a not-yet-live Firestore concern; low is fairer.

title: finalize_run is a non-atomic alias of create_run (INSERT OR REPLACE) — no transactional finalize
evidence: sqlite_backend.py:114-115: 'def finalize_run(self, run): self.create_run(run)' — finalize is identical to create, both doing INSERT OR REPLACE (line 108) of the whole row. firestore_backend.py:73-74 mirrors this: finalize_run just calls create_run, which does a full document .set() (line 71). The port (storage.py:26-29) declares create_run and finalize_run as distinct methods implying distinct semantics, but neither backend does a conditional/transactional finalize (e.g. UPDATE WHERE status='running'). The denormalized status column (sqlite_backend.py:46,51,109) is only ever rewritten by these full-row replaces.
impact: Low in the current single-flight, single-writer model (run_trigger holds a process-wide lock so concurrent finalizes can't race). But the port's create/finalize distinction is decorative — finalize blindly overwrites the entire row, so a stale or out-of-order finalize would clobber a newer state, and there is no atomic 'mark this RUNNING run as SUCCESS' guarantee should the writer model ever change (multiple workers, retries).
recommendation: Either collapse the port to a single upsert_run() to stop implying a transactional finalize that doesn't exist, or make finalize_run a real conditional update (SQLite: UPDATE ... WHERE run_id=? AND status='running'; Firestore: a transaction reading current status). Low priority while single-flight holds, but the API surface currently over-promises.
verifier_notes: All factual claims verified against source. sqlite_backend.py:114-115 — finalize_run(self, run) is literally `self.create_run(run)`, and create_run (line 107-112) does `INSERT OR REPLACE INTO digest_runs (run_id, org_id, status, started_at, data)` i.e. a full-row replace. firestore_backend.py:73-74 mirrors this exactly: finalize_run calls create_run, which does a full document `.set(run.model_dump(mode="json"))` (line 71). The port declares create_run and finalize_run as separate @abstractmethods (storage.py:26,29) — note the finding's cited path was wrong (it's app/core/ports/storage.py, not app/adapters/storage/storage.py) but the content claim holds. Neither backend does any conditional/transactional finalize (no `UPDATE ... WHERE status='running'`); the denormalized status column (line 51) is only ever rewritten by these full-row replaces. Confirmed both call sites do read-modify-write-then-full-replace with no compare-and-set: success path agents.py:442-444 (set status SUCCESS/PARTIAL then finalize_run) and failure path runner.py:191-196 (get_run, mutate to FAILED, finalize_run). Severity 'low' is correctly calibrated and the author is honest about it: run_trigger.py:16,34 uses a process-wide non-blocking threading.Lock so only one run_digest executes per process, eliminating concurrent-finalize races today. The risk is purely latent for a future multi-instance/multi-worker model — which the codebase itself flags as a 'production milestone' (run_trigger.py:5, config_store.py:18). Real, accurately scoped, not overstated.

title: Rubric grades SENTIMENT but neither the runtime critic nor the offline judge is ever shown the sentiment field — the clause is unreachable in both guardrails
evidence: faithfulness_rubric.md line 11 has a dedicated clause: "SENTIMENT APPROPRIATENESS: the stated sentiment (positive/neutral/negative) must be supported by the source tone; treat a clearly unsupported sentiment as a faithfulness problem." But _critic_payload (critic.py:51-64) serializes only {id, title, excerpt, summary_en, summary_ar} — no sentiment. _judge_payload (judge.py:39-57) serializes only {id, title, excerpt, enrichment:{category, importance_score, summary_en, summary_ar}} — no sentiment. Confirmed by grep: "sentiment" appears in neither critic.py nor judge.py nor eval_score.py. So both LLM-as-judge graders are asked to assess a field they never receive; the rubric clause is dead in both the runtime path and the offline eval path.
impact: The single shared rubric (a deliberate, well-engineered design point) silently contains a dimension that can never fire. An unfaithful/inverted sentiment will never be flagged or redacted at runtime, and the offline eval cannot detect sentiment miscalibration despite the rubric implying it does. Quality coverage is narrower than the rubric advertises.
recommendation: Either add the sentiment field to _critic_payload and _judge_payload (and to the judge's EnrichmentVerdict dimensions if you want it scored separately) so the rubric clause is actually exercised, OR delete the SENTIMENT clause from faithfulness_rubric.md so the rubric matches what the graders can see. Pick one — the current state is a silent over-claim.
verifier_notes:
All factual claims verified against the real code.

1) /app/prompts/faithfulness_rubric.md line 11 contains the clause verbatim: "SENTIMENT APPROPRIATENESS: the stated sentiment (positive/neutral/negative) must be supported by the source tone; treat a clearly unsupported sentiment as a faithfulness problem."

2) /app/pipeline/critic.py _critic_payload (lines 51-64) serializes exactly {id, title, excerpt, summary_en, summary_ar} — no sentiment field. The rubric is composed into _CRITIC_PROMPT (critic.py:20-23) so the model SEES the clause but never receives the item's sentiment value.

3) /app/pipeline/judge.py _judge_payload (lines 39-57) serializes {id, title, excerpt, enrichment:{category, importance_score, summary_en, summary_ar}} — no sentiment field. Same rubric composition (judge.py:20-23).

4) grep confirms "sentiment" appears in NEITHER critic.py, judge.py, NOR app/pipeline/eval_score.py. It exists only in processing.py:129 (item.sentiment = enrichment.sentiment), llm/schema.py, core/domain.py (Sentiment enum + NewsItem.sentiment field), excel.py (render), the reprocess/processing prompts, and tests. The Sentiment field is real and populated on items — it is just never handed to either grader.

Corroborating evidence the finding did not cite: eval_schema.py EnrichmentVerdict/FaithfulnessVerdict have NO sentiment dimension, and eval_score.py THRESHOLDS/_DIMENSIONS cover only faithfulness, category_accuracy, importance_calibration, ar_translation_quality. So there is no structured slot to record a sentiment verdict either — reinforcing that the clause cannot fire and is rolled (in intent) into faithfulness, but the grader can't exercise it because the stated sentiment is absent from its input.

The clause IS genuinely unreachable in both the runtime critic path and the offline judge path: the grader is told to assess "the stated sentiment" but the stated sentiment is never present in the payload. (Note: critic.md:29 also name-drops "sentiment" in its skepticism list — same dead-letter problem; the model still has no sentiment value to check.)

Verdict: CONFIRMED. This is real contract-drift in the single shared rubric. Severity downgraded from medium to LOW: impact is a silent narrowing of advertised quality coverage on a secondary metadata field (sentiment), not the primary summary content. The actual summary_en/summary_ar text faithfulness IS fully checked; there is no runtime error, no correctness/security regression, and sentiment was never a scored gate. The defect is a dead rubric clause / coverage gap, appropriately low severity.

title: output_key is set on every LLM agent but never read; structured output_schema coexists with manual parse_model_json with no reconciliation
evidence: Every builder sets output_key (processing.py:57 "processing_result", critic.py:46 "verdicts", judge.py:34 "verdicts", digest_editor.py:36 "digest_narrative", youtube.py:201 "youtube_summary"). grep across app/api, app/pipeline/agents.py, app/runner.py finds zero reads of any of those state keys — the runtime instead re-parses the agent's raw final text with parse_model_json. So output_schema is set on the Agent AND parse_model_json re-parses the same result: two structured-output mechanisms, with the output_key/session-state mechanism entirely unused.
impact: Cognitive noise and a contradictory contract: a reader can't tell whether the structured output_schema (which constrains the model) or the manual text re-parse is load-bearing. Harmless at runtime but it muddies the otherwise-clean LLM layer and invites future bugs if someone starts trusting the never-populated output_key state.
recommendation: Decide on one mechanism. Keep output_schema (it constrains generation) but drop the unused output_key from the builders, and add a one-line comment that parse_model_json is the authoritative read because the runtime consumes the agent's final text, not session state.
verifier_notes:
Finding holds up on direct code inspection.

CLAIM 1 (output_key set but never read) — CONFIRMED. All five builders set output_key: processing.py:57,100 ("processing_result"), critic.py:46 ("verdicts"), judge.py:34 ("verdicts"), digest_editor.py:36 ("digest_narrative"), youtube.py:201 ("youtube_summary"). The only execution path is run_agent_text (llm/runtime.py:83) — wired in as the default pipeline callables in runner.py:96-108 (adk_enrich/adk_narrate/adk_critique/adk_reprocess). _run_text_async (runtime.py:61-80) builds a throwaway InMemoryRunner + session inside the coroutine, returns only event.content.parts[0].text, and discards the session. The session state where output_key lands is never read back. grep across app/ and tests/ finds ZERO session-state reads of any of those four key strings (state["processing_result"], state["verdicts"], state["digest_narrative"], state["youtube_summary"] — all absent). The session-state keys agents.py actually reads are unrelated: "run", "items", "errors_*", "run_id" (agents.py:122,129,136,164,253). Note critic.py:155 "verdicts" is a return-dict literal key and agents.py:360 run.critic_verdicts is a domain attribute — neither is an output_key read.

CLAIM 2 (output_schema coexists with manual parse_model_json) — CONFIRMED. Every builder sets output_schema AND the matching adk_* fn re-parses raw text: parse_model_json (processing.py:66,114; critic.py:71; judge.py:70; digest_editor.py:48) or model_validate_json (youtube.py:211).

SEVERITY: claimed low is correct, not overstated. Genuinely harmless at runtime — output_key is inert dead config. One nuance the auditor slightly overstates as "two structured-output mechanisms with no reconciliation": output_schema constrains the model's generation to emit parseable JSON (generation-side), while parse_model_json is the extraction step (parse-side) — they are complementary, not strictly redundant. The actual defect is narrower: an unused output_key argument repeated across five agent builders = cognitive noise / dead config, a legitimate low-severity duplication/cleanliness issue. Recommend dropping output_key from the five builders (or switching to read-from-state) to make the contract unambiguous.

title: Critic verdicts and flagged counts are persisted but surfaced by no API serializer or UI — the guardrail's audit trail is a dead-end
evidence: GuardrailCriticAgent writes run.flagged and run.critic_verdicts (agents.py:359-360) and they are stored in DigestRun (domain.py:186-187) and persisted in the SQLite blob. grep for "critic_verdicts" / "flagged" across app/api/ and frontend/lib/, frontend/components/ returns nothing — no endpoint or component exposes them. (Consistent with the missing Pipeline screen noted elsewhere.)
impact: The faithfulness guardrail does real, sound work (flag/redact/reflect) but its audit output — why an item was withheld, how many were flagged — is invisible to operators. A user can't see or trust the guardrail, reinforcing the "agents feel disconnected from the app" feeling even though the agent logic is correct.
recommendation: Add run.flagged and a compact critic_verdicts summary to the GET /api/runs/{id} response_model and render a small "N items withheld by faithfulness check" indicator on the digest detail view. Low effort; turns invisible guardrail work into visible product value.
verifier_notes: Core gap is REAL but the wording "surfaced by no API serializer" is overstated. Verified facts: (1) agents.py:359-360 GuardrailCriticAgent sets run.flagged and run.critic_verdicts (also the fail-closed path at 371 sets flagged); domain.py:186-187 declares them on DigestRun, so they persist in the SQLite blob. CONFIRMED. (2) API DOES serialize them. app/api/schemas.py embeds the full DigestRun model in DashboardOut (latest_run/recent_runs) and RunDetail (run); app.py:216-245 uses these as response_model for GET /dashboard and GET /runs/{run_id}. Pydantic emits ALL DigestRun fields, so flagged + critic_verdicts ARE in the raw JSON responses. The grep on app/api/ found nothing only because the fields are inherited via model embedding, not named literally. So the API is NOT a dead-end. (3) The FRONTEND is where the data dies: frontend/lib/schemas.ts:59-72 (digestRunSchema) and frontend/lib/types.ts:34-47 (DigestRun interface) deliberately omit both fields; with Zod v4 default strip behavior (^4.4.3, no .passthrough()), the client drops the keys on parse. Scoped grep of frontend/app, frontend/lib, frontend/components for flagged|critic|verdict|withheld|redact|guardrail|faithful returns zero matches. run-health-card.tsx shows Started/Finished/Pipeline/Source-errors only — no flagged count. So no component renders the guardrail audit trail and the UI never mentions it. Net: operators cannot see how many items were withheld or why — the audit output is invisible in the product. The finding's impact holds; only the attribution ("no API serializer") is inaccurate — it should read "the API serializes them but the frontend schema/UI drops and never renders them." Severity low is correct: this is a true scope-gap (consistent with the missing Pipeline screen), not a correctness or security defect.

title: resolve `name` field is in both contracts and read by the UI but never populated by the backend (dead auto-fill)
evidence: ResolveOut declares channel_id/url/name (app/api/schemas.py:38-42) and the frontend resolveSource return type mirrors it as { channel_id?; url?; name? } (api.ts:147-155). The source form reads it: `if (r.name && form.name.trim() === "") set("name", r.name)` (source-form-dialog.tsx:125-127) to auto-fill the source name on Resolve. But the backend resolve_source handler ONLY ever returns ResolveOut(channel_id=cid) or ResolveOut(url=feed) (app/api/app.py:298,309) — a grep for `name=` in app/api/app.py / youtube_resolve.py / feed_discovery.py shows `name` is NEVER set on a ResolveOut. So r.name is always None.
impact: A user-facing affordance (auto-populate the source Name when you paste & Resolve a YouTube/RSS link) is dead — it silently never fires, so users always type names manually and may assume Resolve is half-broken. It is the kind of small 'doesn't behave as intended' gap that accumulates into the user's overall impression.
recommendation: Either implement it (have youtube_resolve / feed_discovery return a title and set ResolveOut(name=...) so the UI auto-fill works) or remove the dead `name` field from ResolveOut and the resolveSource return type plus the form's name-autofill branch. Don't leave a contract field that one side reads and the other never writes.
verifier_notes: Technically fully confirmed; severity overstated. ResolveOut declares name (app/api/schemas.py:41) and the frontend mirrors it (api.ts:150) and reads it for auto-fill (source-form-dialog.tsx:125-127: `if (r.name && form.name.trim() === "") set("name", r.name)`). The backend NEVER populates it: the only two ResolveOut constructions in the whole repo are ResolveOut(channel_id=cid) (app.py:298) and ResolveOut(url=feed) (app.py:309). The resolver functions cannot supply a name either — the word "name" does not even appear in app/services/youtube_resolve.py or feed_discovery.py; resolve_channel_id returns a channel-id string or None, discover_feed returns a feed URL. A repo-wide grep incl. tests/ finds zero `ResolveOut(name=` / `name=` on a ResolveOut, and `git log -S "ResolveOut(name"` shows it was never set in any past commit. So r.name is always None and the auto-fill branch is dead code. All cited line numbers (schemas.py:38-42, app.py:298/309, api.ts:147-155, dialog:125-127) are accurate. Severity corrected medium->low: impact is a minor convenience auto-fill that no-ops silently (guarded by `r.name &&`, so no crash/error), the dialog remains fully usable by typing the name, and there is no data, correctness, or security consequence. Real contract-drift/dead-affordance, but cosmetic.

title: resolveSource is the only read path not Zod-validated at the boundary
evidence: Every other read goes through request(path, undefined, <zodSchema>) so a malformed body throws ApiError(0) (api.ts:72-82). resolveSource calls request("/api/sources/resolve", { method: "POST", body }) with NO schema arg (api.ts:151-154), so the response is returned via the unchecked `return json as T` branch (api.ts:84) and blind-cast to { channel_id?; url?; name? }.
impact: Low: the backend response_model=ResolveOut guarantees the shape server-side, so in practice it's safe. But it's an inconsistency in the otherwise-uniform validate-at-the-boundary discipline; if the backend ever changes ResolveOut, the frontend won't fail loudly the way every other endpoint does.
recommendation: Add a resolveOutSchema (z.object({ channel_id: z.string().nullish(), url: z.string().nullish(), name: z.string().nullish() })) in schemas.ts and pass it as the third arg to the resolveSource request() call for parity with the other read paths.
verifier_notes:
The mechanical mechanism is accurate but the headline claim ("resolveSource is the ONLY read path not Zod-validated") is false.

Verified in frontend/lib/api.ts: request<T>() only validates when a schema arg is passed (lines 72-82); otherwise it returns `json as T` unchecked (line 84). resolveSource (lines 147-155) indeed passes no schema and blind-casts to {channel_id?; url?; name?}. Backend mitigation is real: app/api/app.py:283 declares @api.post("/sources/resolve", response_model=ResolveOut), and app/api/schemas.py:38 defines ResolveOut, so the shape is guaranteed server-side. There is no resolveOutSchema in frontend/lib/schemas.ts.

REFUTATION of "ONLY": getSettings() in api.ts (lines 157-163) is a genuine read path — it maps to backend @api.get("/settings") (app/api/app.py:170) — and it calls request("/api/settings") with NO schema, hitting the same unchecked `return json as T` branch and blind-casting to {app_host; app_port; gemini_key_set; shadowed_keys}. No settings schema exists in schemas.ts. So at least one other read (GET) path also bypasses boundary validation; resolveSource is not unique.

Secondary nuance: resolveSource is actually an HTTP POST (mutation verb), so calling it a "read path" is itself loose; getSettings is the cleaner counterexample of an unvalidated GET read. putSources/putWatchlist/triggerRun/putSettings also lack schemas but are writes, which the finding correctly excludes.

Net: the contract-drift observation (validate-at-the-boundary is applied inconsistently) is genuine and minor, but the specific "only read path" claim is wrong. Severity correctly low — server-side response_model on the resolve endpoint means no practical safety gap, and the inconsistency is cosmetic/discipline-level.

title: DigestRun.flagged and critic_verdicts are returned by the API but absent from the frontend schema (harmless strip)
evidence: DigestRun carries flagged:int and critic_verdicts:list[dict] (app/core/domain.py:186-187). GET /api/runs and GET /api/news have NO response_model (app/api/app.py:232-237, 247-253), so FastAPI serializes the FULL Pydantic objects including flagged/critic_verdicts; the response_model'd routes (/dashboard, /runs/{id}) embed DigestRun directly and also include them. The frontend digestRunSchema (schemas.ts:59-72) lists neither field. Because it's a plain z.object (non-strict), Zod silently strips the extra keys rather than failing.
impact: No runtime break — Zod tolerates the extra keys. But it confirms the guardrail/critic audit data (the count of redacted items and the per-item verdicts) reaches the API yet has no frontend schema or screen to land in — the same 'missing Pipeline screen' gap the mapping phase flagged, seen from the contract side. The dual no-response_model routes also mean the OpenAPI/typed contract for /runs and /news is implicit rather than declared.
recommendation: If a Pipeline/guardrail view is ever built, add flagged + a CriticVerdict schema to digestRunSchema and surface them. Independently, add explicit response_model=list[DigestRun] / list[NewsItem] to GET /api/runs and GET /api/news (app/api/app.py:232,247) so the wire contract is declared and the frontend schema is provably the source of truth, not an after-the-fact mirror.
verifier_notes:
Every cited fact verified against the source.

1. app/core/domain.py:186-187 — DigestRun(BaseModel) declares `flagged: int = 0` and `critic_verdicts: list[dict] = Field(default_factory=list)`. Exact match.

2. app/api/app.py:232-237 (GET /runs) and :247-253 (GET /news) have NO response_model and `return storage().list_runs(...)` / `list_news(...)` directly. FastAPI therefore serializes the full DigestRun/NewsItem Pydantic objects, including flagged and critic_verdicts. Confirmed.

3. The response_model'd routes do embed DigestRun: /dashboard (app.py:216, response_model=DashboardOut) and /runs/{id} (app.py:239, response_model=RunDetail). app/api/schemas.py:10-19 shows DashboardOut.latest_run/recent_runs are DigestRun and RunDetail.run is DigestRun, so flagged/critic_verdicts flow through these too. Confirmed.

4. frontend/lib/schemas.ts:59-72 — digestRunSchema is a plain z.object listing run_id, org_id, started_at, finished_at, status, collected, new, processed, high_importance, outputs, source_errors, narrative — neither flagged nor critic_verdicts. No .strict() or .passthrough() anywhere in frontend/lib/*.ts. Zod v4 (package.json: "zod":"^4.4.3") default object behavior strips unknown keys rather than failing. Confirmed.

5. frontend/lib/api.ts:73 uses schema.safeParse(json) and returns result.data, so extra keys are silently stripped with no runtime break. Confirmed.

6. grep confirms `flagged`/`critic_verdict` appear nowhere in frontend/lib (no type, no schema, no screen).

Severity low is correct and not overstated — the finding itself frames it as a harmless strip with no runtime break, which matches reality. It is legitimate contract drift: guardrail/critic audit data (redaction count + per-item verdicts) reaches the API but has no frontend schema or screen to consume it. The secondary observation — that the two no-response_model routes (/runs, /news) leave the OpenAPI/typed contract implicit rather than declared — is also accurate.
