title: Scope creep is finished, not half-done: YouTube collector, desktop app, and the News screen are complete
evidence: Spec listed 4 source types; a 5th (YouTube) was added and is fully wired through the single dispatch chokepoint (runner.py:90-91 dispatches SourceType.YOUTUBE -> youtube.collect; agents.py:77 includes it in COLLECTED_SOURCE_TYPES) and tested (tests/unit/test_youtube.py 9.9K, test_youtube_resolve.py). A 'News' browser screen not in the spec's 8 screens exists (frontend/app/news/page.tsx, 172 lines, real SWR data + filters). The macOS desktop single-port launcher (scripts/run.sh, build/Catch-Up.app) is complete and matches its own 2026-06-20 spec. None of these are stubs.
impact: The creep is genuinely shipped and adds value, so it is not the source of brokenness. It does, however, widen the gap between spec and reality — the team built unplanned surfaces while leaving three spec'd screens (Categories/Pipeline/Schedule) unbuilt.
recommendation: Reconcile the spec to reality: add YouTube (already in ARCHITECTURE.md), the News screen, and the desktop app to the canonical scope; then either build or formally drop Categories/Pipeline/Schedule so the spec stops overselling. The problem is prioritization drift, not unfinished creep.
verifier_notes:
Every factual code claim in the finding holds up against the source.

YouTube is a real 5th source type, fully wired and tested:
- app/core/domain.py:13-18 — SourceType has exactly 5 members: RSS, SCRAPE, API, SEARCH (the 4 originals) + YOUTUBE.
- app/runner.py:90-91 — _collect dispatches SourceType.YOUTUBE -> youtube.collect(source, settings, storage=storage). Exact match to the cited lines.
- app/pipeline/agents.py:72-78 — COLLECTED_SOURCE_TYPES tuple includes SourceType.YOUTUBE; this single source-of-truth drives both the per-type ParallelAgent collectors (build_pipeline lines 487-500) and the NormalizeDedup merge (lines 249-253). agents.py:77 is the YOUTUBE entry.
- app/services/youtube.py — 285 lines, non-stub: channel-feed fetch (SSRF-guarded via safe_get/resolve_channel_id), feedparser parsing, multi-language transcript fallback (ar/en...), an LLM summarizer agent, and a dedup-aware collector. No placeholders.
- tests/unit/test_youtube.py (9.9K) and test_youtube_resolve.py (2.8K) both exist on disk.

News screen is real and not in the claimed 8-screen spec set:
- frontend/app/news/page.tsx — 172 lines, real SWR data via useNews(), category/importance/limit filters, loading skeletons, empty/error boundaries. Functional, not a stub.

Desktop launcher is complete:
- scripts/run.sh — 167 lines (6.0K): dotenv port read, is-our-app health probe, next-free-port picker, cold-start lock with dead-owner reclaim, build-if-missing console, detached uvicorn with health-wait, chromeless Chromium window. Production-grade.
- build/Catch-Up.app/Contents/MacOS/run — 2-line exec wrapper pointing at scripts/run.sh. Real.

Spec-vs-reality gap is consistent with disk: frontend/app has exactly 5 route dirs (digests, news, settings, sources, watchlist). The finding's claimed-unbuilt Categories/Pipeline/Schedule screens are indeed absent.

Caveat (why not refuted, but why category is debatable): the 'over-engineering' label is a value judgment, not a defect. The COLLECTED_SOURCE_TYPES design was deliberately built so adding a source type is a one-line change, and the feature is tested and cleanly integrated. The finding itself correctly states this is NOT the source of brokenness and rates it low severity — that self-assessment is honest. Severity low is appropriate; nothing here is broken or risky, it is purely a scope-discipline observation.

title: Markdown renderer does not escape titles, URLs, or source names, allowing injection if rendered to HTML
evidence: markdown.py:39 interpolates item.title/url/source_name raw into Markdown link syntax and :41 the summary raw. Those come from untrusted third-party RawItem content. Unlike html.py (html.escape + safe_href per field), markdown applies no escaping and no http(s) href allowlist; a javascript-scheme URL or link metacharacters pass verbatim.
impact: Low today (file artifact), but Markdown is a transport format: once rendered to HTML (email/docs/viewer) the unescaped title/url is stored XSS or link injection. Inconsistent trust model vs the HTML renderer.
recommendation: Escape Markdown metacharacters in title/source_name and run item.url through html.py's safe_href allowlist before interpolation.
verifier_notes:
Verified accurate on every point. app/services/render/markdown.py:39 raw-interpolates item.title, item.url, and item.source_name into Markdown link syntax `- [{title}]({url}){badge} — *{source_name}*`, and :41 raw-interpolates item.summary_en. No escaping, no http(s) href allowlist anywhere in the file.

Untrusted-source premise holds: NewsItem.title/url/source_name are copied verbatim from RawItem via NewsItem.from_raw (domain.py:162-164). RawItem is built by collectors from external feeds — e.g. app/services/rss.py:60-71 takes entry.link (url) and entry.title directly from feedparser output with only .strip() on the title, no scheme validation. summary_en is LLM-generated over that same external content. So a malicious/compromised feed can inject a `javascript:` URL or Markdown/link metacharacters that pass through to the rendered output.

Inconsistent trust model confirmed: the sibling html.py defends every field — _esc() = html.escape (lines 37-38, applied at 57/59/60/89) and _safe_href() collapses any non-http(s) scheme to '#' with an explicit docstring naming javascript:/data: injection (lines 41-49). markdown.py applies none of these. Even RawItem.image_url has a collector-side http(s) validation comment (domain.py:128), but url/title get no such guarantee.

Severity correctly scoped as low and not overstated: render_markdown is consumed by write_markdown, which only writes a .md file to disk (markdown.py:46-51) — there is no active HTML sink in this codebase today, so it is not live XSS now. The risk is conditional on the Markdown later being rendered to HTML by a downstream consumer (email/docs/viewer), which the finding explicitly acknowledges. Real, latent, low.

title: output_key set on every LLM agent but never read — pure dead writes
evidence: output_key is set on 6 agents (processing.py:57,100 'processing_result'; critic.py:46 'verdicts'; judge.py:34 'verdicts'; digest_editor.py:36 'digest_narrative'; youtube.py:201 'youtube_summary'). The only consumer of these agents is run_agent_text -> _run_text_async (runtime.py:61-80), which harvests the result from event.content.parts[0].text (runtime.py:74-75) and never reads session.state[output_key]. grep for any read of these keys in app/ returns zero hits outside the writes. ADK writes output_key into session.state, but the InMemoryRunner session here is created per-call (runtime.py:65-66) and thrown away; nothing ever inspects it.
impact: Misleading contract: a reader assumes downstream stages consume processing_result/verdicts/digest_narrative from state, but the real data path is text re-parsing via parse_model_json. Combined with the output_schema-AND-manual-parse redundancy, it obscures which mechanism is load-bearing.
recommendation: Either remove output_key from all six agents (the text path is the real one), OR commit to reading from session.state and drop the manual parse_model_json re-parse. Pick one mechanism. Lowest-risk: delete the output_key kwargs.
verifier_notes:
Finding is accurate. All six output_key values are confirmed at the cited lines: processing.py:57 and :100 ("processing_result"), critic.py:46 ("verdicts"), judge.py:34 ("verdicts"), digest_editor.py:36 ("digest_narrative"), youtube.py:201 ("youtube_summary").

Data path verified: every adk_* function (adk_enrich, adk_reprocess, adk_critique, adk_judge, adk_narrate, adk_summarize) calls run_agent_text(agent, payload, settings) and then re-parses the returned TEXT via parse_model_json / DigestNarrative.model_validate_json. run_agent_text -> _run_text_async (runtime.py:64-76) creates a fresh InMemoryRunner + session per call, harvests text from event.content.parts[0].text (line 75), and returns the string; the session (where ADK would write output_key into session.state) is local to _consume() and discarded. session.state is never read in that function.

grep across app/ confirms zero reads of these keys from state. The only non-declaration hits are agents.py:360 (run.critic_verdicts = outcome["verdicts"]) and critic.py:155 (outcome dict construction) — both operate on a plain Python dict returned by apply_verdicts/reflect_and_correct, NOT session.state. The separate session-state layer in agents.py uses entirely different keys (items, run_id, errors_{key}) and reads via _read_run/_read_items.

Architecture confirmation: the SequentialAgent tree (build_pipeline, lines 502+) wires custom BaseAgent subclasses (ProcessingAgent, CriticAgent, DigestEditorAgent, etc.) as sub_agents, NOT the LLM agents that carry output_key. Those LLM agents are built inside _default_* factories and invoked via asyncio.to_thread on the plain functions, never added to an ADK agent tree where output_key state propagation would matter. So the output_key writes land in throwaway sessions and are genuinely dead.

Severity: claimed "low" is correct — pure maintainability/clarity issue, zero functional impact, one redundant kwarg per agent.

One correction to the IMPACT wording: the "output_schema-AND-manual-parse redundancy" framing is slightly off. output_schema constrains the model's structured output, and parse_model_json/model_validate_json parse the returned JSON text — that pair is the actual load-bearing mechanism, not redundant. Only output_key is the dead write. The core finding (dead output_key writes, misleading contract that downstream reads from state) stands.

title: build_pipeline run_id is an unused 'forward compatibility' hook; agent.py generates a run_id at import that is thrown away
evidence: build_pipeline's own docstring (agents.py:471-472) says: 'The build_pipeline run_id param is accepted for forward compatibility but unused here — PipelineInitAgent reads it from ctx.session.state.' The param (agents.py:457) is never referenced in the function body. Meanwhile agent.py:25 calls build_pipeline(..., run_id=uuid.uuid4().hex[:12]) generating a run_id at module import that is immediately discarded; PipelineInitAgent regenerates its own (agents.py:164-168).
impact: A no-op parameter that exists only to be ignored — textbook unused forward-compat hook. It misleads callers into thinking they can control the run_id through the factory, and the agent.py call wastes an import-time uuid.
recommendation: Remove the run_id parameter from build_pipeline entirely (run_id is seeded via session state) and drop the run_id kwarg at agent.py:25. If a future use is real, add it when it's real, not speculatively.
verifier_notes:
All three core claims verified against the actual code.

1. build_pipeline's run_id param IS unused. Signature: agents.py:457 `run_id: str | None = None`. The function body (agents.py:474-543) never references it — `grep -n "run_id"` over agents.py shows the only mentions inside build_pipeline are the docstring lines (466, 468, 471). The docstring at agents.py:471-472 explicitly states: "The build_pipeline `run_id` param is accepted for forward compatibility but unused here — PipelineInitAgent reads it from ctx.session.state."

2. agent.py:25 calls `build_pipeline(_settings, build_storage(_settings), run_id=uuid.uuid4().hex[:12])` at module import. Since build_pipeline discards the arg and agent.py never seeds any session state with it, this import-time uuid is thrown away. PipelineInitAgent (agents.py:164-168) reads run_id from ctx.session.state and only falls back to its own `uuid.uuid4().hex[:12]` when absent — which is exactly what happens for the agent.py-constructed root_agent.

3. The real seeding mechanism confirms the param's redundancy: runner.py:118-120 does `create_session(..., state={"run_id": run_id})` — that is how run_id actually reaches PipelineInitAgent. Notably even the legitimate caller run_digest (runner.py:165-167) passes `run_id=run_id` to build_pipeline redundantly, then separately seeds it via session state. So the param is a no-op for every caller.

Severity correctly rated low / over-engineering: this is not a correctness bug (PipelineInitAgent's fallback and runner.py's session seeding both work correctly). The actual harm is a misleading API surface (callers reasonably assume the factory's run_id controls the run) plus one negligible wasted import-time uuid. It is a genuine textbook unused forward-compat hook, accurately described and not overstated. Relevant files: app/pipeline/agents.py (453-473, 160-176), app/agent.py (25), app/runner.py (116-198).

title: agent.py does side-effecting DB schema init + watchlist load at module import time
evidence: agent.py:24-26 runs at import: _settings = Settings(); root_agent = build_pipeline(_settings, build_storage(_settings), run_id=...). build_storage (runner.py:62) calls store.init_schema() — a SQLite CREATE TABLE/ADD COLUMN write. build_pipeline (agents.py:482) calls load_watchlist (file read). So merely importing app.agent (which playground/eval/ADK discovery does) writes to the DB and touches config files.
impact: Import-time side effects make the module non-inert: tooling that imports app.agent for introspection creates/migrates a SQLite file. This is also a second source-of-truth for the pipeline (root_agent vs run_digest), duplicating construction.
recommendation: Move root_agent construction behind a lazy factory (a function) or guard with a module-level cache that does no I/O at import. At minimum, don't call init_schema at import time.
verifier_notes:
All technical claims verified against the real code and empirically reproduced.

CHAIN CONFIRMED:
- app/agent.py:24-26 runs unconditionally at module scope (no __main__ guard): _settings = Settings(); root_agent = build_pipeline(_settings, build_storage(_settings), run_id=uuid...); app = App(...).
- app/runner.py:52-62 build_storage() calls store.init_schema(); and SqliteBackend.__init__ (sqlite_backend.py:19-21) also does Path(path).parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) — a filesystem write.
- app/adapters/storage/sqlite_backend.py:42-63 init_schema() executes CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS (news_items, digest_runs), ALTER TABLE ... ADD COLUMN (column migrations), and CREATE INDEX — genuine DB writes.
- app/pipeline/agents.py:482 build_pipeline() calls load_watchlist(settings.config_dir), which (watchlist.py:26-31) reads config/watchlist.yaml.
- Defaults (config.py:81-83): storage_backend='sqlite', sqlite_path=REPO_ROOT/data/catchup.db, config_dir=REPO_ROOT/config — so import with defaults writes to the repo's data dir.
- ADK does import this module for discovery: vendored cli_deploy.py:571 errors unless agent.py exports root_agent or app, confirming playground/eval/deploy import app.agent.

EMPIRICAL PROOF: With a temp SQLITE_PATH, `import app.agent` (no run invoked) turned a non-existent DB file into one containing tables ['digest_runs','news_items']. So merely importing the module materializes + migrates a SQLite schema. EVIDENCE is accurate.

WHY SEVERITY DOWNGRADED to low (claimed medium is overstated):
- The DDL is fully idempotent (IF NOT EXISTS / conditional ADD COLUMN); it creates an empty local schema, never destructive, no data loss, no network for the default sqlite backend.
- Constructing root_agent at module import is the standard ADK scaffold pattern (ADK itself expects the export), so the 'non-inert module' part is conventional, not real drift. The genuinely surprising bit is the schema/file write riding along inside build_storage — that's a legitimate but low-impact smell, easily fixed by lazy storage init.
- Secondary 'duplicate source-of-truth' impact is real but minor: agent.py and run_digest (runner.py:146-172) both call the shared build_pipeline factory, so it's duplicated invocation, not duplicated logic; the import-time root_agent is not used by run_digest.
- Note: with storage_backend='firestore', build_storage would instead construct a live Firestore client at import (heavier side effect), but that is not the default.

Relevant files (absolute): app/agent.py; app/runner.py (build_storage L52-62); app/adapters/storage/sqlite_backend.py (init_schema L42-63, __init__ L19-21); app/pipeline/agents.py (L482); app/services/watchlist.py (L26-31); app/core/config.py (L81-83).

title: NewsItem.language field is declared but never assigned or read anywhere
evidence: domain.py:152 declares `language: str | None = None`. grep across app/ and tests/ for any assignment or read of NewsItem .language (excluding summary_lang / use_language / target_lang) returns only the declaration. No collector, processor, renderer, API, or frontend ever sets or reads it.
impact: Pure dead field. Suggests a planned (and dropped) per-item language tag that never landed; adds noise to the domain model and the API payload.
recommendation: Delete NewsItem.language. If per-item language is wanted later, add it together with the producer that populates it.
verifier_notes:
Confirmed. app/core/domain.py:152 declares `language: str | None = None`. It is the only backend reference: NewsItem.from_raw (domain.py:156-170) does NOT set it, and the LLM enrichment schema ItemEnrichment (app/llm/schema.py:8-19) has no `language` field, so the processor never populates it. A clean grep over app/ (excluding license boilerplate, the unrelated youtube transcript `language_code`/`language` getattr in app/services/youtube.py, and the ```json fence comment in app/llm/parse.py) returns ONLY the declaration. No assignment or setattr anywhere. The field is therefore always None.

One nuance the auditor missed (which makes the impact slightly larger, not smaller, so the finding is if anything understated on reach but correct in substance): the field IS serialized. NewsItem.model_dump(mode="json") is used in app/pipeline/agents.py:141/145, app/adapters/storage/firestore_backend.py:48, and app/adapters/storage/sqlite_backend.py:88, so an always-null `language` is written into stored docs and the API payload. The frontend even mirrors it: frontend/lib/types.ts:28 has `language: string | null;` and frontend/components/digests/news-card.test.tsx:30 sets `language: "en"` in a fixture. BUT the renderer frontend/components/digests/news-card.tsx never reads item.language — the only `.language` read in frontend source (excluding .next build artifacts and node_modules) is an unrelated type-fest declaration. The display-language behavior uses the useLanguage() UI preference, not the per-item field. So it is genuinely a dead, always-null field that adds noise to the domain model, the serialized payload, and the mirrored TS type.

Severity low is correct: a single always-null optional field is trivial — no correctness or security impact, purely model/payload noise consistent with a dropped per-item language-tag idea.

title: DigestRun.flagged and critic_verdicts are computed and persisted but dead end-to-end (no UI consumer)
evidence: agents.py:359-360 set run.flagged and run.critic_verdicts from the guardrail outcome; domain.py:186-187 persist them. They are serialized in RunDetail (run is dumped whole), but the frontend digestRunSchema (schemas.ts:59-72) does NOT include flagged or critic_verdicts, and Zod strips unknown keys at api.ts's parse boundary — so they never reach any component. No Pipeline/guardrail screen exists to render them. grep finds zero references to critic_verdicts/flagged in app/api/schemas.py response models, frontend/lib, or frontend/components.
impact: The guardrail's entire audit output (which items were flagged and the per-id verdicts) is computed every run, stored, then silently dropped at the frontend. Wasted persistence and a symptom of the missing Pipeline screen.
recommendation: Either surface flagged/critic_verdicts (add to the frontend run schema + a minimal guardrail panel) or, if the Pipeline screen is being dropped from v1 scope, stop persisting critic_verdicts (keep just the flagged count) to avoid storing data nothing reads.
verifier_notes: Every claim verified against source. (1) app/pipeline/agents.py:359-360 sets run.flagged = outcome["flagged"] and run.critic_verdicts = outcome["verdicts"] (plus line 371 sets run.flagged in the fail-closed exception path). (2) app/core/domain.py:186-187 declares them as DigestRun fields: `flagged: int = 0` and `critic_verdicts: list[dict] = Field(default_factory=list)`. (3) They ARE serialized: app/api/schemas.py:17-19 RunDetail.run is the full DigestRun, and app/api/app.py:240-245 returns RunDetail(run=run, items=...) so both fields appear in the /api/runs/{run_id} JSON. (4) frontend/lib/schemas.ts:59-72 digestRunSchema is a plain z.object (Zod default = strip unknown keys; no .passthrough()) and omits both fields. (5) frontend/lib/api.ts:72-81 runs schema.safeParse(json) and returns result.data for getRun/listRuns/getDashboard, so the stripped object is what reaches React. (6) frontend/lib/types.ts:34-47 DigestRun interface also omits both fields, so no component can reference them. Clean grep over frontend/lib, frontend/components, frontend/app, and app/api found ZERO references to critic_verdicts or flagged (the prior auditor's grep was polluted by node_modules; a filtered grep confirms none in source). The only 'pipeline/guardrail' hit was a static UI label "Pipeline" in dashboard/run-health-card.tsx:37 — not a data consumer. run-detail.tsx renders nothing from these fields. Confirmed dead end-to-end: computed every run, persisted, serialized, then silently dropped at the Zod parse boundary. Severity 'low' is correct — no security/functional impact; it is wasted persistence and a symptom of the missing Pipeline/guardrail screen, exactly as reported.

title: Dead ADK-native eval scaffold targeting a deleted weather/greeting agent sits beside the real harness
evidence: basic.evalset.json (lines 6-35) contains eval_cases 'greeting' ('Hello, what can you help me with?') and 'weather_query' ('What's the weather like in San Francisco?') against app_name 'app' — the deleted scaffold conversational agent. The current root_agent is a SequentialAgent news pipeline (agent.py:25), not a conversational weather bot, so these cases cannot run meaningfully. eval_config.json defines generic relevance/helpfulness rubrics for that conversational path. The real eval harness (scripts/eval_enrichment.py + app/pipeline/eval_score.py) is a completely different batch enrichment judge. grep shows nothing in app/ references basic.evalset/eval_config.
impact: Two contradictory eval philosophies in one tests/eval/ tree with no signpost which is real. Anyone running `agents-cli eval` gets a harness pointed at a non-existent agent. This is concrete 'scattered' cruft.
recommendation: DELETE basic.evalset.json, eval_config.json, and evalsets/README.md. The custom enrichment harness is the real one; keep it and add a one-line note in docs/eval/README.md that the ADK-native eval path is intentionally not used.
verifier_notes:
Core facts CONFIRMED. tests/eval/evalsets/basic.evalset.json (lines 6-35) contains exactly the eval_cases 'greeting' ("Hello, what can you help me with?") and 'weather_query' ("What's the weather like in San Francisco?"), both targeting app_name "app". git log --follow shows this file came from commit 8c9747a "chore: scaffold ADK project at repo root (prototype, AI Studio)" — i.e. untouched scaffold cruft. The real root_agent is a SequentialAgent news pipeline: app/agent.py:25 calls build_pipeline (app/pipeline/agents.py:453) which returns a SequentialAgent (agents.py:502). grep confirms ZERO references to weather/greeting/"San Francisco" anywhere in app/. eval_config.json defines generic relevance/helpfulness rubrics with a gemini-flash-latest judge. The real enrichment harness (scripts/eval_enrichment.py + app/pipeline/eval_score.py) is a genuinely separate batch judge keyed on tests/eval/fixtures/enrichment_reference.json and tests/eval/baseline.json (dimensions: faithfulness, category_accuracy, importance_calibration, ar_translation_quality) and never references basic.evalset.json or eval_config.json. WHY DOWNGRADED from high to low: (1) The IMPACT claim "harness pointed at a non-existent agent" is INACCURATE/overstated. app/agent.py registers App(root_agent=root_agent, name="app"), so the agent named "app" DOES exist — agents-cli eval would route these cases to the REAL news SequentialAgent. The cases are meaningless (a news pipeline fed "what's the weather" yields nonsense judged by generic rubrics), not unrunnable against a deleted agent. (2) Impact is confined to test tooling and reviewer/maintainer confusion ("two eval philosophies, no signpost") — a real hygiene/dead-code issue, but with no correctness, runtime, CI, or security consequence. The project's actual eval path is pytest + eval_enrichment.py's --check-regression against baseline.json, which is wired and works. This is legitimate scaffold cruft worth deleting, but it is low severity, not high. (Side note: app/runner.py:117 registers the pipeline under app_name="catchup", a separate inconsistency not central to this finding.)

title: reference_enrichment branch in eval_enrichment.py is permanently dead (fixture never has the key)
evidence: eval_enrichment.py:126 reads `ref_dict = case.get('reference_enrichment', case['gold'])`. The fixture cases only have keys ['item','gold','expectations'] (verified by loading enrichment_reference.json) — 'reference_enrichment' is absent from the file entirely, so the branch always falls back to case['gold']. eval_score.py:161 docstring also references the never-present key.
impact: Dead conditional plus stale doc text describing a feature (separate reference vs gold) that is not wired. Minor, but it's the kind of speculative branch that adds confusion.
recommendation: Simplify to `ref_dict = case['gold']` and drop the reference_enrichment mention from the docstring (eval_score.py:161), unless you actually intend to populate reference_enrichment in the fixture.
verifier_notes:
Factual core is correct: scripts/eval_enrichment.py:126 does `ref_dict = case.get("reference_enrichment", case["gold"])`, and the fixture tests/eval/fixtures/enrichment_reference.json contains zero `reference_enrichment` keys (grep across all .py/.json confirms only the code/docstring mention it). All 30 fixture cases have exactly keys [item, gold, expectations], so the .get() always falls back to case["gold"]. eval_score.py:161 docstring does mention `reference_enrichment`. So the "branch never takes the first value today" part is TRUE.

However, the finding's framing is overstated, which is why this is PARTIAL not confirmed. This is NOT a "dead conditional" or "speculative branch that adds confusion" — it is a deliberately documented optional-override hook. Lines 124-125 carry an explicit comment: "reference_enrichment is optional; gold already encodes the known-good / known-bad input that the judge must score, so fall back to it." The run_calibration docstring (lines 110-113) likewise states "reference_enrichment if present, else the gold enrichment". `dict.get(key, default)` with an intentionally-optional key and a working, documented fallback is idiomatic Python, not dead code — the calibration path works correctly today via the gold fallback. The eval_score.py:161 docstring is describing the conceptual reference input being fed to the judge, not advertising a wired-but-broken feature; calling it "stale doc" is a stretch.

Net: severity stays at the claimed "low" as an upper bound, but the impact ("dead code", "confusion", "stale doc text describing a feature that is not wired") is exaggerated. It is at most a minor cleanliness nit (an unused optional override), and arguably a legitimate intentional extension point that should not be flagged at all.

title: item.status is a free-string with magic values duplicated across four files instead of an enum
evidence: domain.py:153 declares status as a plain str. The value set 'raw'/'processed'/'filtered'/'flagged' is hard-coded as string literals in processing.py:132,165; critic.py:129,134,147; agents.py:369. There is no single enum; the same magic strings are duplicated across stages.
impact: A typo in any of these literals silently produces a bogus status with no error; the valid set is implicit and undiscoverable. The other enums in the domain (Category, Sentiment, SourceType) are proper StrEnums — status is the odd one out.
recommendation: Introduce a StrEnum ItemStatus (RAW/PROCESSED/FILTERED/FLAGGED) in domain.py and replace the seven string-literal assignments with the enum. Eliminates the duplication and makes the valid set explicit.
verifier_notes:
All cited lines verified. app/core/domain.py:153 declares `status: str = "raw"` as a plain free-string (the only such status field; DigestRun.status at line 178 uses the RunStatus StrEnum). The literal set raw/processed/filtered/flagged is hard-coded as string literals at the cited sites: processing.py:132 ("processed"/"filtered"), 165 ("raw"); critic.py:129,134,147 ("flagged"); agents.py:369 ("flagged"). No ItemStatus enum exists anywhere (domain.py defines 6 other StrEnums: SourceType, Category, Importance, Sentiment, EntityType, RunStatus), so the auditor's "odd one out" framing is accurate.

Finding is actually UNDERSTATED in scope: the magic strings also appear in files NOT cited, including READ/comparison sites that drive routing and storage logic — agents.py:431 (`i.status == "processed"`), runner.py:113 (`i.status == "processed"` ... `i.status != "flagged"` for selection), adapters/storage/firestore_backend.py:49 (`item.status == "flagged"`), and sqlite_backend.py:100,145 (raw SQL `status != 'flagged'`). So 7+ files, both write and read sites.

Severity correctly stays LOW: this is a latent maintainability/data-integrity smell, not a live defect. All current literals are spelled consistently; no actual typo or mis-route exists today. A future typo at a write site would silently mis-route an item through selection/storage filters rather than raise — that latent risk justifies low (arguably low-to-medium given the read sites feed routing), but not higher since nothing is currently broken.

title: Four un-cached Settings() constructions, two at import time — no single config entrypoint
evidence: Settings() is constructed independently at agent.py:24 (import time), fast_api_app.py:50 (import time), runner.py:160 (per run_digest call, as fallback), and api/app.py:324 (per create_app call, as fallback). There is no @lru_cache/get_settings() factory; pydantic-settings re-reads env/dotenv each time.
impact: Config is re-parsed in multiple places and two of them run at import, which is the mechanical source of the 'config is everywhere' feeling. Risk of subtle divergence if env shadowing changes between constructions during a process's life.
recommendation: Add a single cached `get_settings()` (functools.lru_cache) in app/core/config.py and route all four call-sites through it. Removes the import-time constructions and gives one source of truth.
verifier_notes:
Mechanical facts are all TRUE and verified at the exact cited lines:
- app/agent.py:24 `_settings = Settings()` — import time.
- app/fast_api_app.py:50 `_settings = Settings()` — import time.
- app/runner.py:160 `settings = settings or Settings()` — fallback inside run_digest().
- app/api/app.py:324 `settings = settings or Settings()` — fallback inside create_app().
There is NO @lru_cache and NO config factory. The lone get_settings() (app/api/app.py:171) is an HTTP route handler (GET /api/settings) returning a status dict, not a settings factory — so "no single config entrypoint" is literally correct.

But the IMPACT is overstated, so partial:
1. Two of the four (runner.py:160, api/app.py:324) are NOT unconditional re-parses — they are `settings or Settings()` dependency-injection defaults behind a `settings: Settings | None = None` param. In normal flow settings is injected and propagated (create_app -> lifespan/scheduler/run_digest); Settings() only constructs when nothing is passed (tests, standalone CLI entry). Calling these "re-parsed in multiple places" overstates it.
2. The two import-time constructions live in two DISTINCT, mutually-exclusive deployment entrypoints that are not both loaded in one process: agent.py + fast_api_app.py are the ADK managed-runtime surface (explicitly documented at fast_api_app.py:15-29), while api/app.py create_app() is the product REST API run by `catchup serve`. So at most one import-time Settings() runs per process surface.
3. The claimed "divergence if env shadowing changes" risk is the exact hazard the code already defends against: detect_env_shadow() (config.py:38) is invoked in create_app() at app/api/app.py:328 and logs a loud warning when root .env shadows app/.env. Settings reads from a fixed env_file=("app/.env",".env") (config.py:78); values freeze at construction.

Net: factually accurate, low severity is the correct ceiling (arguably borderline informational). Confirmed as a real "no shared get_settings() factory" observation; refuted as a meaningful correctness/divergence risk.

title: Deploy descriptor (manifest) contradicts every production claim in the docs
evidence: agents-cli-manifest.yaml — the ONLY machine-readable deploy descriptor — carries stock scaffold defaults: deployment_target='none' (line 9), session_type='in_memory' (line 10), cicd_runner='skip' (line 11), datastore='none' (line 14), region='us-east1' (line 4). Meanwhile README.md:10-11 advertises 'scales to Google Cloud (Cloud Run + Cloud Scheduler + Vertex AI + Firestore) by configuration' and README.md:18 says these are 'wired and unit-tested'. There is no terraform/infra/deployment directory anywhere (find for *.tf returns nothing), so the `agents-cli infra single-project` path the CLAUDE.md guide references has produced no committed artifacts.
impact: If anyone runs `agents-cli deploy`, the manifest provisions a deployment target of none, in-memory sessions, and no datastore — i.e. NONE of the documented prod stack — so the deploy either no-ops or stands up an ephemeral container with no persistence. The manifest and the README describe two different products. region us-east1 also contradicts the project's own GOOGLE_CLOUD_LOCATION='global' default (config.py:116) and the CLAUDE.md model-404 guidance.
recommendation: Either update the manifest to realize the documented prod path (deployment_target=cloud_run, datastore=firestore, region=global or a real region, and add the terraform via `agents-cli infra`), or — the honest minimal fix — set deployment_target=none AND change README/ARCHITECTURE/ADK-GUIDE to state the GCP path is design-only / not built. Stop presenting the manifest as a working deploy descriptor while it provisions nothing.
verifier_notes:
The raw manifest facts are all accurate, but the finding's central thesis ("contradicts every production claim", "two different products") is refuted by the docs themselves.

CONFIRMED facts: agents-cli-manifest.yaml does carry stock scaffold defaults — region:"us-east1" (L4), deployment_target:"none" (L9), session_type:"in_memory" (L10), cicd_runner:"skip" (L11), datastore:"none" (L14). No *.tf files exist anywhere in the repo (verified with find). config.py:116 does set google_cloud_location:"global".

WHY OVERSTATED / REFUTED THESIS: The README does NOT advertise a working production deployment. README.md:14-19 explicitly labels the project "Beta", says only "The local, single-user app runs end-to-end today", and states the cloud paths "are wired and unit-tested but **not yet validated against live GCP**." README.md:10-11 says the architecture "scales to Google Cloud ... **by configuration**" — a future-capability claim, not a "this is deployed" claim. docs/ADK-GUIDE.md:165 is even more explicit: "Deploy: the milestone target is local/self-hosted; the App (Agent Engine / Cloud Run) path is **future**." ADK-GUIDE.md:158 separately flags Firestore as "**not validated against live Firestore**". So docs and manifest AGREE: local today, cloud later. There is no contradiction between the manifest's "none" defaults and an honestly-scoped beta that disclaims live-GCP readiness.

"wired and unit-tested" (README:18) is selectively quoted — it refers to the port/adapter code swap-points (SQLite->Firestore, AI Studio->Vertex), which the same sentence scopes as "not yet validated against live GCP." That matches reality.

IMPACT framing collapses: CLAUDE.md Phase 5 requires explicit human approval before `agents-cli deploy`, and no doc claims the deploy is production-ready, so "the manifest provisions none of the documented prod stack" describes an expected pre-deploy scaffold state, not a deception.

VALID-BUT-MINOR sub-point: region "us-east1" (deploy region, an unreviewed scaffold default) sits oddly next to the project's stated GOOGLE_CLOUD_LOCATION "global" preference (config.py:116). But these are different axes — us-east1 is a deploy/runtime region; "global" is the genai/Vertex model-call endpoint chosen to avoid model-404s. Not a strict contradiction, and moot since no live deploy has occurred. This is a config-hygiene nit worth one cleanup line, not a high-severity deployment defect.

Net: real underlying observation (stale scaffold defaults, no committed terraform) but the high-severity "contradicts the docs / two different products" narrative is refuted. Downgrade to low.

title: Two ASGI entrypoints / two ports / two session stores — the structural root of the 'scattered' feeling
evidence: Desktop/serve path: scripts/run.sh:136 boots `uvicorn app.api.app:create_app --factory` on 127.0.0.1:8000 — console + /api/*, SQLite DatabaseSessionService, scheduler lifespan (app.py:337-349). Deploy path: Dockerfile:39 boots `uvicorn app.fast_api_app:app` on 0.0.0.0:8080 — ADK agent web UI + /api/* re-mount, in-memory sessions, NO scheduler lifespan, NO console. The scheduler (build_scheduler) is wired ONLY in create_app's _lifespan (app.py:339); fast_api_app.py has no lifespan, so even APScheduler-based scheduling is desktop-only. The two apps assemble routes differently and are mutually unaware.
impact: Two deploy surfaces with different storage, sessions, scheduling, and console behavior. There is no single 'this is how the product runs' answer — which is precisely the user's complaint. A reader cannot tell whether the agent pipeline, the SQLite product DB, and the web console are one system or three.
recommendation: Designate ONE canonical entrypoint. Recommended: app.api.app:create_app (it already serves console + /api/*, durable sessions, and scheduler) and containerize THAT (build frontend/out into the image, run on $PORT). Keep the ADK get_fast_api_app surface ONLY if an Agent-Engine/ADK-web deployment is a real requirement; otherwise delete fast_api_app.py to remove the second door. One app, one port, one session store.
verifier_notes:
Mixed: the literal entrypoint/port facts hold, but the framing ("two session stores", "different storage", "mutually unaware", "can't tell if one system or three") is overstated/wrong. CONFIRMED facts: - scripts/run.sh:136-137 boots `uvicorn app.api.app:create_app --factory --host 127.0.0.1 --port

`; port defaults to 8000 (read_port, line 39). Serves console + /api/*. - Dockerfile:39 boots `uvicorn app.fast_api_app:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8080`. Serves ADK web UI + /api/*. - Scheduler (build_scheduler) is wired ONLY in create_app's _lifespan (app/api/app.py:337-349). fast_api_app.py has no lifespan, so APScheduler in-process scheduling is serve-path-only. - Console mount (mount_console) is create_app-only (app/api/app.py:366-367); fast_api_app.py has no console. REFUTED / OVERSTATED: - "Mutually unaware": FALSE. fast_api_app.py imports and calls register_product_routes(app, _settings) (lines 38, 77) — the SAME function create_app calls (app/api/app.py:358). Both surfaces deliberately share the entire /api/* route layer. Both module docstrings explicitly document the relationship and intent (fast_api_app.py:15-29; app/api/app.py:132-140). This is documented, intentional design, not silent drift. - "Two session stores" / "different sessions": CONFLATED. fast_api_app.py:58 session_service_uri=None affects only the ADK developer WEB-UI CHAT sessions, not the product. The agent PIPELINE session store is chosen by make_session_service(settings) in app/runner.py:74-78 from settings.session_backend (default "database" -> SQLite sessions.db, config.py:106) and is IDENTICAL regardless of which ASGI app runs, because /api/runs -> run_digest is the same code path in both apps. - "Different storage": FALSE. The product DB (build_storage, storage_backend="sqlite", config.py:81-82) is the same in both apps via the shared routes. Net: real, documented two-surface design (managed-runtime ADK agent wrapper vs standalone product API) sharing the product route layer and the product/pipeline storage. The genuine deltas are scheduler and static console being serve-only — a minor, defensible split, not the "no single answer for how the product runs" the finding claims. Severity downgraded medium -> low.

title: The deployed container entrypoint (app/fast_api_app.py) is never directly tested — prod artifact has only proxy coverage
evidence: test_deploy_surface.py:4-9 explicitly states it does NOT import app/fast_api_app.py ('it calls google.auth.default() and creates a Cloud Logging client at import time, which needs GCP creds. We test register_product_routes ... instead'). Grep confirms no test does `from app.fast_api_app import ...`. So the actual ASGI app the Dockerfile boots (`uvicorn app.fast_api_app:app`) — including its hardcoded in-memory sessions and its no-frontend behavior — is verified only by analogy to create_app.
impact: The two-entrypoint split (desktop create_app vs Docker fast_api_app) is the exact 'agents vs gcp webapp scattered' seam the user complains about, and the deployed half is the untested half. A divergence between register_product_routes-as-mounted-on-a-bare-app and the real fast_api_app assembly (CORS, session backend, auth wiring) would not be caught.
recommendation: Add a thin test that imports fast_api_app under monkeypatched google.auth.default / Cloud Logging (or guards those at import behind a lazy/env check) and asserts /api/health + one product route respond, plus asserts the session backend it actually configures. This closes the gap between 'routes are mountable' and 'the shipped container serves them correctly'.
verifier_notes:
Core claim CONFIRMED. app/fast_api_app.py is the deployed entrypoint (Dockerfile:38 `uvicorn app.fast_api_app:app`) and is never imported by any test. test_deploy_surface.py:5-8 explicitly states it avoids the import because the module calls google.auth.default() and creates a Cloud Logging client at module top-level (fast_api_app.py:44-46). Grep verified: zero non-self imports of app.fast_api_app in the repo; tests/conftest.py does NOT stub google.auth/Cloud Logging, so the module genuinely can't be imported under test as written. The /feedback endpoint (fast_api_app.py:80-91) is the only project-authored route unique to this module and has no test coverage (the only "feedback" grep hit is unrelated critic logic). get_fast_api_app, session_service_uri=None, and the ADK web UI are exercised by no test.

WHERE THE FINDING OVERSTATES (-> partial, severity down to low): The product /api/* business logic AND its auth (_require_api_key, app.py:93-111) live entirely inside register_product_routes, which IS tested (mounted on a bare FastAPI in test_register_product_routes_mounts_api_on_any_app). So the genuinely untested delta is thin glue: (1) ADK library config passed as one-liners (allow_origins, session_service_uri) into an external library, (2) the 8-line /feedback endpoint, (3) the interaction of mounting register_product_routes on ADK's real app vs a bare app. The finding's "silent divergence in routes" framing is weak because fast_api_app.py:26-28 documents the two surfaces share NO routes by design. One nuance the finding gets right: CORS is wired by two DIFFERENT mechanisms (create_app uses its own CORSMiddleware app.py:352-357; fast_api_app delegates to get_fast_api_app's CORS at line 66), so the deployed CORS is not even verified by true analogy.

Net: a real, accurately-described coverage gap, but it covers thin glue code blocked by an import-time-creds limitation, with the substantive logic already tested via the shared function. Medium overstates it; low is fair.

title: Two ADK-scaffold modules (telemetry, typing) are entirely untested dead-ish weight in the package
evidence: Grep across tests/ for 'app_utils', 'telemetry', and 'Feedback' returns 0 references (excluding pycache). These are Google scaffold leftovers shipped in the importable package with no coverage and, per the mapping, no live callers in the product path.
impact: Low — they are small and not on the runtime path, but they inflate the package surface and any latent bug in them is invisible. Minor erosion of the 'every file is tested' confidence the suite otherwise earns.
recommendation: Either delete them if the deployed ADK surface does not require them, or add a trivial import/smoke test and keep them. Prefer deletion to keep the package honest.
verifier_notes:
The "dead-code" framing is REFUTED; only the narrow "untested" sub-claim survives, so PARTIAL at most.

What the code actually shows:
- app/app_utils/telemetry.py defines setup_telemetry(), which is imported AND called at module-load in the deployed FastAPI entrypoint: app/fast_api_app.py:39 (import) and :43 (setup_telemetry()). It configures OpenTelemetry GenAI prompt-response logging keyed off LOGS_BUCKET_NAME / OTEL_* env vars — real runtime behavior on the deployed container, not dead weight.
- app/app_utils/typing.py defines the Feedback pydantic model, imported at app/fast_api_app.py:40 and used as the request body of the LIVE POST /feedback endpoint (app/fast_api_app.py:80-91), which calls logger.log_struct(feedback.model_dump(), severity="INFO"). docs/superpowers/plans/2026-06-17-critical-medium-remediation.md confirms /feedback is part of the deployed surface.

So the EVIDENCE claim "no live callers in the product path" and the IMPACT claim "not on the runtime path" are both factually wrong. There IS exactly one live caller for each, in the deployed app entrypoint.

The ONLY accurate part: grep in tests/ for app_utils/telemetry/Feedback/setup_telemetry returns 0 hits (confirmed, excluding __pycache__), and there is no indirect coverage either — tests/integration/test_deploy_surface.py:5-8 explicitly documents that it deliberately does NOT import fast_api_app.py (because it calls google.auth.default() and creates a Cloud Logging client at import time, needing GCP creds), testing register_product_routes/create_app instead. So both modules genuinely lack test coverage.

Net: this is a minor test-gap, not dead code. Category should be test-coverage, not dead-code. Severity stays low (these are tiny, but they DO run in production, which if anything makes the untested-ness slightly more notable than "dead weight"; still low). Keeping low.

title: Stale 2026-05-23 spec contradicts shipped product (tree, source types, screens, API)
evidence: README line 21 links it as approved design. Spec sec21 tree backend catchup core usecases ports scheduler llm adapters scheduling - flat app has none (ports only storage, scheduler is app services scheduler.py). FR2 four types, SourceType RSS SCRAPE API SEARCH - domain lines 13-18 has five (YOUTUBE). sec17 eight screens incl Categories Pipeline Runs - about six exist. sec17 routes digests categories pipeline - app api app.py 164-289 none. sec18 config app.yaml absent (grep empty; config has only sources.yaml watchlist.yaml).
impact: The doc presented as the authoritative approved design misdescribes tree, sources, UI, config, and API - a concrete source of the scattered not-as-intended feeling.
recommendation: Add a dated superseded as-built deltas addendum pointing to the later 2026-06 specs, or stop linking it as the current design and label it a historical first cut.
verifier_notes:
Every concrete factual claim is TRUE — verified against code:

1) TREE (spec sec21): spec describes backend/catchup/... with core/usecases/, core/ports/{storage,scheduler,llm}, adapters/scheduling/. Reality: NO backend/ dir (code is flat at repo root under app/); NO usecases/ dir; app/core/ports/ has only storage.py (no scheduler.py, no llm.py); NO app/adapters/scheduling/ dir; scheduler is at app/services/scheduler.py. Domain is app/core/domain.py, not core/domain/schema.py. Confirmed.

2) SOURCE TYPES (FR2 / sec7): spec says 4 (RSS, SCRAPE, API, SEARCH). Code app/core/domain.py:13-18 has 5 — adds YOUTUBE. Confirmed.

3) SCREENS (sec17): spec lists 8 incl Categories, Pipeline, separate Runs&Schedule. Reality: 6 frontend routes (dashboard=app/page.tsx, news, digests, sources, watchlist, settings). No Categories/Pipeline/Runs screens. Confirmed.

4) API (sec17): spec lists GET/POST /api/digests[/run], CRUD /api/categories, GET/PUT /api/pipeline/config. app/api/app.py:141-289 registers none of these; actual routes: /api/health, /api/settings (get/put), /api/dashboard, /api/runs (get/{id}/post), /api/news, /api/sources (get/put), /api/watchlist (get/put), /api/sources/resolve. Confirmed.

5) CONFIG (sec18): spec references config/app.yaml. No app.yaml exists anywhere; config/ has only sources.yaml + watchlist.yaml; settings come from env/.env via app/core/config.py. Confirmed.

WHY PARTIAL / SEVERITY DOWNGRADE: the finding's IMPACT framing is overstated. The README does NOT present this dated spec as the live authoritative design. README:19-21 names ARCHITECTURE.md FIRST ('see ARCHITECTURE.md and the design spec'); README:252-262 ('How it was built — kept for learning') explicitly frames docs/superpowers/specs/ as a deliberate historical/build artifact: 'kept deliberately, as a learning resource and an honest record of how the project was built... not required to run or use Catch-Up — feel free to ignore or delete them.' So drift in an intentionally point-in-time doc is expected and disclosed, not a hidden contradiction in the authoritative design. Minor residual issue: the spec's own header still reads 'Status: Approved' with no as-built disclaimer inside the file, and README:21 'Design is approved — see ... the design spec' can read as endorsing it as current — so a small doc-drift note is warranted, hence low not refuted.

title: Deploy descriptors contradict documented prod architecture
evidence: README 18-19 says cloud paths wired; ARCHITECTURE 127 says DatabaseSessionService. But manifest lines 9 11 14 deployment target none session in memory datastore none. fast_api_app 58 67 session service uri None (ephemeral). Dockerfile 23 27 39 copies only app and config (no frontend out), runs fast_api_app.
impact: Deployed container has ephemeral sessions and no console; durable Firestore console code is wired only to the local run_digest serve path, not the deploy artifact - the literal agents gcp webapp sqlite scattered mismatch.
recommendation: Wire fast_api_app manifest Dockerfile to the prod path (session uri, datastore, copy frontend out), or downgrade README ARCHITECTURE to local only.
verifier_notes:
The finding's headline is largely refuted; only a narrow, documented sub-claim survives.

REFUTED — "durable Firestore console code wired only to local serve path, not deploy artifact": False. app/fast_api_app.py:77 calls register_product_routes(app, _settings). Those /api/* routes (dashboard, runs, news, sources, watchlist) call storage() -> build_storage(settings) (app/api/app.py:157-158, 218-253), and build_storage selects FirestoreBackend when STORAGE_BACKEND=firestore (app/runner.py:52-62). So the durable SQLite->Firestore product-storage path IS present in the deploy artifact and is env-driven. The console reaches the same storage code in the container as locally.

REFUTED/MISATTRIBUTED — "ephemeral sessions": session_service_uri = None at fast_api_app.py:58 governs ONLY the ADK web-UI/playground session surface from get_fast_api_app — not product storage and not the digest pipeline. The actual digest runs (CLI, scheduler, POST /api/runs) go through run_digest -> make_session_service (runner.py:74-78,173), which defaults to DatabaseSessionService (session_backend default = "database", config.py:106). That is exactly what ARCHITECTURE.md:127 documents ("ADK sessions via DatabaseSessionService (SQLite)"). No contradiction.

CONTEXT — manifest "deployment_target: none / session_type: in_memory / datastore: none" (agents-cli-manifest.yaml:9,10,14) are google-agents-cli scaffold-generation metadata (generated_at 2026-05-23), not the live runtime config. Runtime is driven by Settings/env vars.

CONFIRMED (narrow, minor) — Dockerfile copies only ./app (line 23) and ./config (line 27), not frontend/out, and fast_api_app.py never calls mount_console (only create_app does — app/api/app.py:364-367 gated on console_dir). So the deployed container serves the REST API but NOT the static Next.js console UI. This is real but minor and explicitly documented as a not-yet-validated future state: README:18-19 ("cloud paths ... wired and unit-tested but not yet validated against live GCP") and docs/ADK-GUIDE.md:165 ("milestone target is local/self-hosted; the App (Agent Engine / Cloud Run) path is future").

Net: severity downgraded high -> low. The deploy artifact is NOT ephemeral and DOES carry the durable storage path; the only valid gap is the missing static console bundle in the image, which is a deliberate, documented state for an unshipped cloud path.

title: env.example documents 4 of about 38 settings
evidence: env.example lines 1-17 has only GOOGLE_API_KEY GNEWS_API_KEY API_KEY ALLOW_ORIGINS. config.py exposes about 38 settings. README 166-174 highlights APP_PORT LLM_MODEL STORAGE_BACKEND SESSION_BACKEND USE_VERTEXAI GOOGLE_CLOUD_PROJECT GOOGLE_CLOUD_LOCATION SCHEDULE vars - none present. Spec sec18 257 promised env.example documents all variables.
impact: The self-host starting point omits the port Vertex Firestore scheduler swap-points the README advertises; discoverable only by reading source.
recommendation: Add commented defaults for the README-highlighted vars to env.example.
verifier_notes:
Facts are accurate. app/.env.example (lines 1-17) documents exactly 4 vars: GOOGLE_API_KEY, GNEWS_API_KEY, API_KEY, ALLOW_ORIGINS. app/core/config.py Settings class exposes 37 fields (lines 80-162) — the "about 38" claim is correct. The README swap-point vars (APP_PORT, LLM_MODEL, STORAGE_BACKEND, SESSION_BACKEND, USE_VERTEXAI, GOOGLE_CLOUD_PROJECT, GOOGLE_CLOUD_LOCATION, SCHEDULE_*) are indeed absent from .env.example. The spec promise is real: docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-23-adk-catchup-agent-design.md:257 says verbatim "`.env.example` documents all variables." So the spec-vs-implementation gap is genuine.

Why downgraded to low (impact overstated): (1) The impact claim that these swap-points are "discoverable only by reading source" is false — README.md lines 162-176 contain a Configuration reference table that explicitly documents GOOGLE_API_KEY, GNEWS_API_KEY, APP_PORT, LLM_MODEL, STORAGE_BACKEND, SESSION_BACKEND, USE_VERTEXAI, GOOGLE_CLOUD_PROJECT/LOCATION, API_KEY, ALLOW_ORIGINS, and SCHEDULE_ENABLED/CRON/TIMEZONE, then says "See app/core/config.py for the complete list." So the port/Vertex/Firestore/scheduler vars the impact says are hidden are in fact advertised in the README. (2) .env.example's own header ("Copy to app/.env and fill in. NEVER commit... Rotate any key") shows it is scoped as a secrets template, and all 4 entries are keys/security settings — an appropriate scope. (3) No runtime/security impact; every setting has a working default. This is a doc-completeness gap (broken spec promise) substantially mitigated by the README, hence low rather than medium.

title: Manifest region us-east1 contradicts global-location guidance and model-404 advice
evidence: manifest line 4 region us-east1. config.py 116 google_cloud_location global with comment that global avoids model-404s on regional endpoints. ADK-GUIDE 157 default global. CLAUDE.md says fix 404s with global instead of us-east1.
impact: agents-cli deploy infra would provision in the region the project explicitly calls 404-prone for the model - a baked-in future deploy failure.
recommendation: Set the manifest region consistent with the global-location guidance and reconcile alongside the in-memory datastore-none cleanup.
verifier_notes:
All four cited facts verified literally true. agents-cli-manifest.yaml line 4: region: "us-east1". app/core/config.py line 116: google_cloud_location: str = "global", with line 113 comment "'global' location avoids the model-404s seen on regional endpoints." docs/ADK-GUIDE.md line ~157 documents Vertex default location "global". CLAUDE.md line 51: "Model 404 errors: Fix GOOGLE_CLOUD_LOCATION (e.g., global instead of us-east1)."

However the IMPACT ("baked-in future deploy failure") is materially overstated, so partial + severity downgraded to low:

1) The two values are DIFFERENT settings. Manifest `region` is the agents-cli infra/provisioning region (where Cloud Run / Agent Engine resources would be created). config.py `google_cloud_location` is the genai/Vertex model-serving endpoint location. The 404 guidance is about the model endpoint, not the provisioning region. They are configured independently; provisioning infra in us-east1 does not force the genai client to call a us-east1 model endpoint. The finding conflates the two.

2) No deploy is wired. Manifest declares deployment_target: "none" (and create_params.deployment_target: "none"). There are no .tf files anywhere in the repo (find -name "*.tf" returns nothing). ADK-GUIDE explicitly states: "the milestone target is local/self-hosted; the App (Agent Engine / Cloud Run) path is future." So `agents-cli deploy`/`infra` is not currently a supported path.

3) Vertex is disabled. use_vertexai defaults to False (config.py line 114); the app runs on AI Studio via GOOGLE_API_KEY (app/.env, ADK-GUIDE section 6). The regional-endpoint 404 code path is not even active.

4) No app/script code reads the manifest region (grep across app/ and scripts/ finds only unrelated "region" entity-type usages). The field is consumed only by agents-cli tooling at a future deploy step.

Conclusion: a real but minor cosmetic/latent inconsistency — a leftover scaffold default (us-east1) that doesn't match the project's documented preference for global. Worth a one-line cleanup, but not a medium-severity baked-in deploy failure given deploy is unwired, Vertex is off, and region vs location are distinct knobs.

Side note (out of scope for this finding): app/.env contains what appear to be live GOOGLE_API_KEY and GNEWS_API_KEY values; .env is gitignored so likely not committed, but flagging separately.

title: sources.yaml example.com scrape placeholder is non-functional
evidence: sources.yaml lines 48-55 example_scrape url example.com news selector a.headline enabled false - not a real news page and a.headline matches nothing there.
impact: Disabled so harmless to the out-of-box run, but a dead template that yields zero items with no obvious cause if naively enabled.
recommendation: Replace with a real working disabled example or remove it from the committed defaults.
verifier_notes: Evidence verified against config/sources.yaml lines 48-55: the example_scrape source is exactly as described — type scrape, url https://example.com/news, selector a.headline, category_hint business_finance, enabled false. example.com is the IANA-reserved illustration domain serving a static placeholder page (no /news path, no a.headline elements). Cross-checked the scrape handler at app/services/scrape.py: parse_page (lines 37-59) runs soup.select(source.selector); against the example.com page this matches nothing, the for-loop body never executes, and collect() returns an empty list. So if naively enabled it would silently produce zero items, matching the IMPACT claim. However, the finding is partially mitigated by an in-file comment on line 48 ('# Example scrape source — set a real selector before enabling.') that explicitly flags it as a configurable template, so the cause is not entirely non-obvious. It is also enabled: false out-of-the-box (line 55), so it is harmless to the default run. This is a deliberate, clearly-labeled template placeholder rather than accidental dead code. Severity 'low' is correct; arguably it could even be cosmetic given the explanatory comment, but low is defensible. Confirmed but benign.
